body: QuestionPro Customer Experience (CX) — Product Overview Source: Internal QP Wiki (product/customer-experience-home) Stakeholder & Owner: Ken Peterson Last Updated: February 20, 2026 WHAT IS QuestionPro CX? QuestionPro CX is a feedback management platform that transforms insights into actionable data, empowering companies to build stronger customer relationships. Core philosophy: "Understanding Journeys & Taking Action" - Understand customer journeys and take meaningful action - Delight customers at every touchpoint and turn them into brand advocates - Make data-driven decisions to exceed experiences THE WHY (Product Vision): Customers are the lifeblood of a business. Customer experience shapes opinions — not just of the customer, but everyone they share with, physically and virtually. These opinions dictate when, where, why, and how often customers spend money. CX is the foundation of profitability and business health. Customers deserve to be heard. Listening shows you care, laying the groundwork for loyalty. A little empathy goes a long way — apologies and thank-yous are too rare. We believe in empowering all employees to resolve issues quickly and efficiently. Great CX software doesn't require digging through data — it brings opportunities to your attention. It learns, predicts, and helps you deliver great experiences. PRODUCTS IN THE CX SUITE: - Customer Experience (CX Core) — Surveys, NPS, CSAT, CES - Enhanced Closed Loop (eCLF) — Ticket-based issue resolution - Outerloop (Strategic CLF) — Strategic initiative tracking - CX Reputation — Online reputation management - CX Digital Intercept — Website/digital feedback capture - Customer Journey Management (CJM/CJO) — Journey mapping & management - BI in CX (Coming Soon) DEMO ACCOUNT: - URL: www.questionpro.com - Login: cxdemo@questionpro.com | Password: questionpro REGULAR MEETINGS: - CX Townhall: Second Thursday of every month (mandatory for CX employees) - CX Open Office Hours: Tuesdays 10:30–11:30 AM PT (drop-in, optional) - CX Product Discussion: Tuesdays 9:00–9:30 AM PT (invitation only) - Weekly CX Sales/Marketing Sync: Mondays 10:30–11:00 AM PT (invitation only) CX PRODUCT QUARTERLY HIGHLIGHTS: Q1 2023: Disposition Metrics 2.0, NPS+ Delight, Grid dashboard layout, Hierarchical segments Q2 2023: Push metrics, Notifications revamp, Comments list widget, Weighted mean widget Q3 2023: Enhanced closed-loop integration, Word cloud widget, Cross Tab widget, Outerloop integration Q4 2023: API responses in timezone, Custom SMS, Sentiment score trend chart, Dashboard PDF download Q1 2024: Heatmap enhancements, CSV export email report, Survey tokens revamp, Restricted PII access Q2 2024: KPI comparison widget, Centralized root causes, FTP enhancements, Comment list widget update Q3 2024: Notification enhancements, AskWhy edit & retake, Overall UI cleanup, Updated Overall widget Q4 2024: Survey Tokens from survey questions, CLF 1.0 deprecation, Bulk QR download, Centralized Rootcauses & AskWhy Q1 2025

body: QuestionPro CX — Enhanced Closed Loop (eCLF) Source: Internal QP Wiki (product/customer-experience-home/cx-product-page/closedloop) Stakeholder: Ken Peterson | Owner: Anmol Sahetya WHAT IS IT? The Enhanced Closed Loop (eCLF) is QuestionPro's ticket-based customer issue resolution system. When a customer gives low feedback scores or negative responses, the eCLF automatically creates a ticket and routes it to the appropriate team for follow-up and resolution. KEY FEATURE HIGHLIGHTS BY QUARTER: Q2 2026: - CLF Root Cause Actions Q1 2026: - Automatic Escalation Workflows — Time-bound rules to automatically escalate tickets once elapsed time is reached. Supports multi-tiered escalation setups. Q4 2025: - Intuitive Ticket Status Board — Interactive Kanban-style view where agents drag and drop tickets between status columns (New, Open, Escalated, Resolved). Streamlines ticket status management. Q3 2025: - Mandatory Ticket Resolution Checklist — Enforces completion of critical validation steps before a ticket can be resolved. Ensures reliability, consistency, and auditability of resolutions. Improves internal accountability. - AI Generated Tags in Tickets — AI automatically classifies new support tickets based on content using LLMs. Reduces manual effort, improves triage speed and accuracy, and aids trend identification. Q2 2025: - AI Response Assist — Automates customer response management. Provides intelligent, personalized replies to customer feedback. Enables faster responses and improved support quality. Q4 2024: - Notifications Revamp - Disabled manual sync option for managers - Activity History log enhancement (shows managers/admins who made updates) Q3 2024: - PathosAI integration for Sentiment Analysis - Custom variable filter - Bulk ticket import for external tickets - Feature locks - Updated From email (cxtickets@cx-email.com) Q2 2024: - Activity logs added per ticket (under each ticket and under Manage tab) - Ticket URL added to email notifications - Sorting for tickets on ticket list page - Search tickets by ID, email, name, description Q1 2024: - Ticket and Survey information in all notification emails - Customer Phone number added to Customer Details - Overdue filter added in default eCLF filters - SMTP enhancement (From Email Address and Name) - Winback Surveys TEAM: - Anmol Sahetya — Product Manager - Samsul Alam, Ilias Shah, Abu Mohammad Musa — Developers

body: QuestionPro CX — Customer Journey Management (CJM) Source: Internal QP Wiki (product/customer-experience-home/cx-product-page/journey-management) Stakeholder: Ken Peterson | Owner: Anmol Sahetya Last Updated: February 20, 2026 WHAT IS IT? Journey Management is the practice of designing, monitoring, and continuously improving customer journeys using real data, insights, and operational signals. Unlike traditional journey MAPPING (one-time visual diagrams), QuestionPro Journey Management treats journeys as LIVING, EVOLVING SYSTEMS that reflect how customers actually move through experiences. KEY POSITIONING: Journey Management = Journey Mapping + Real Data + Continuous Improvement + Operational Signals DEMO ACCOUNT: - URL: https://journeymanagement.questionpro.com/ - Login: cxdemo@questionpro.com | Password: questionpro KEY DEMO WORKSPACES (use in demos): - Insurance: Track to Purchase, Member Journey, Billing Overview, Parcours Commercial - Grocery Retail: In Store Transaction, Online Merchandise - Airlines: High Level Flying Experience - Bank: Credit Card Application - Sport: High Level Journey, Online Merchandise - Auto: Purchase Journey, Book Car - Manufacturing: Basic Manual Order - Business Services: Accounting User Journey WORKSHOP RESOURCES: - Short Journey Management Workshop: 60–120 minutes, suitable for cross-company audiences - Full Scale Journey Management Training: Used as QuestionPro CJM Certification course (40-question quiz available) - Both workshops have downloadable/customizable presentations (do not edit master) SALES RESOURCES: - CJM One Pager (for printing) - CJM Intro Presentation (adapted from analyst presentation, Feb 2026) - CJM Workshop Presentation - Ready-To-Sell Document - Help files TEAM: - Gargi Ghamandi — Product Manager - Ani Badalyan — Project Manager

summary: QuestionPro CX — Satellite Products Source: Internal QP Wiki (cx-product-page sub-pages) Stakeholder: Ken Peterson | Owner: Gargi Ghamandi CX DIGITAL INTERCEPT What it does: Captures feedback at specific moments in a customer's digital journey — on websites, mobile apps, or other digital touchpoints
body: QuestionPro CX — Satellite Products Source: Internal QP Wiki (cx-product-page sub-pages) Stakeholder: Ken Peterson | Owner: Gargi Ghamandi CX DIGITAL INTERCEPT What it does: Captures feedback at specific moments in a customer's digital journey — on websites, mobile apps, or other digital touchpoints. Surveys pop up contextually based on user behaviour (e.g., on exit intent, time on page, specific pages visited). Use cases: Website experience feedback, digital journey understanding, conversion optimization, UX research. Team: Gargi Ghamandi (PM), Samsul Alam, Asif Ribath, Shakil Ahmed (Dev), Ankita Yadav (QA) CX REPUTATION What it does: Monitors and manages online reputation by aggregating reviews from platforms like Google, Yelp, TripAdvisor, etc. Enables teams to respond to reviews, identify trends, and take action on reputation signals. Use cases: Multi-location businesses, hospitality, retail, healthcare — any org that cares about online review presence. Team: Gargi Ghamandi (PM), Samsul Alam, Asif Ribath, Shakil Ahmed (Dev), Ankita Yadav (QA) CX OUTERLOOP (Strategic CLF) What it does: The Outerloop is the strategic counterpart to the Closed Loop. Where eCLF resolves individual customer issues (tactical), Outerloop identifies systemic themes across feedback and drives strategic product/process improvements at the organizational level. Think of it as: eCLF = fix this customer's problem; Outerloop = fix the root cause so it doesn't happen again. CX CORE METRICS SUPPORTED: - NPS (Net Promoter Score) - CSAT (Customer Satisfaction Score) - CES (Customer Effort Score) - Custom KPIs

body: QuestionPro BI — Product Reference Source: Internal QP Wiki (product/bi-formerly-xa) Stakeholder: Kartik Bhat WHAT IS IT? QuestionPro BI is QuestionPro's data visualization platform. A BI tool developed natively by QuestionPro, designed to consume data from different QuestionPro products. Easy to use, yet extremely powerful. Key differentiator: Unlike third-party BI tools, QuestionPro BI is purpose-built to understand survey/CX/EX data structures out of the box — no complex ETL required. body: QuestionPro Sales Engineering (SE) — Roles, Process & Engagement Guide Source: Internal QP Wiki (sales/sales-architect-se + sales/sales-architect-se/process-guide) Global SE Lead: Kartik Bhat WHAT IS AN SE? A Sales Engineer (SE) is someone with deep understanding of QuestionPro systems who helps bridge the technical gap for Account Executives (AEs) and Account Managers (AMs). Learning the entire software typically takes 6–12 months; SEs accelerate this for sales reps. SE TEAM & COVERAGE: - Kartik Bhat — Global Lead - Yogeshwar Lagad — Research & Insights (APAC, Rest of World) | 9 AM – 6 PM IST - Debashish Gahan — Research & Insights (US, EU, MENA) | 6 PM – 3 AM IST - Aditya Gopalkrishnan — Customer Experience (Global) | 12 PM – 12 AM IST (For CX, only one resource — request 72 hours to 1 week in advance for non-standard hours) WHEN TO LOOP IN AN SE: - Commercial accounts: Must be a Team Edition lead AFTER 1 demo completed - Research, CX, and EX: Any research lead qualifies - Discovery call notes are MANDATORY before SE engagement - A dedicated Slack channel for the deal is MANDATORY WHAT THE SE WILL DO: - Lead the product demo - Answer follow-up technical questions - Assist with COMPLEX parts of RFPs only (Sharon Nau is primary RFP POC) WHAT THE SE WILL NOT DO: - Pricing negotiations - Follow-ups with leads - POC work over 1 hour of effort (that follows the Services process, not SE) AE RESPONSIBILITIES BEFORE AN SE CALL: - 10-minute briefing call with SE for complex deals - Set floor and agenda for the meeting - Share notes to the Slack channel at least 24 hours before the meeting - Record the meeting and share it on the Slack channel HOW TO ENGAGE AN SE: 1. Create a Slack channel and add all notes and key deal details 2. Invite se@questionpro.com to the meeting 3. Keep se@questionpro.com in CC until deal is won or lost DISCOVERY CALL — INFORMATION TO SHARE WITH SE: Customer Details: - Company Name, Industry/Vertical, Number of Employees, Geography Business Needs: - Primary Goal (what does the customer want to achieve with QuestionPro?) - Pain Points (low response rates, lack of analytics, ineffective research?) Technical Requirements: - Integrations needed (CRM, email marketing, etc.) - Data security/compliance needs (GDPR, HIPAA?) - Customization needs (white-labelling, custom surveys?) - Target audience (employees, customers, buyers?) Competitive Context: - Current tools in use and their limitations Demo Focus: - Key features to highlight (reporting, audience segmentation, custom surveys?) SE DEMO PREPARATION CHECKLIST: - Review discovery call notes and understand pain points - Identify key decision-makers and their technical level - Set up demo environment with customer-specific config (branding, templates) - Test any integrations or third-party systems - Prepare responses to technical objections (security, integration, scalability) - Prepare customer-specific use cases - Have relevant case studies or success stories ready - Post-demo: prepar

summary: QuestionPro — Integrations Reference Guide Source: Internal QP Wiki (other-departments/customers/knowledgeinformation-center/integrations) GOOGLE SHEETS INTEGRATION What it does: Export survey responses directly into Google Sheets in real-time. Automatically creates new sheets and maintains up-to-da
body: QuestionPro — Integrations Reference Guide Source: Internal QP Wiki (other-departments/customers/knowledgeinformation-center/integrations) GOOGLE SHEETS INTEGRATION What it does: Export survey responses directly into Google Sheets in real-time. Automatically creates new sheets and maintains up-to-date records without manual entry. Setup Steps: 1. Go to Survey → Integration → Google Sheets 2. Click "Sign in with Google" and authorize the account 3. Select an existing sheet or create a new one 4. Set toggles as needed and click Create Important — Google Drive Scope Update: QuestionPro no longer supports the /drive scope (per Google policy). Now uses /drive.file scope (more secure — only accesses files created through the integration). After reconnecting: Create a new Google Sheet from within QuestionPro; existing sheets can no longer receive pushes. FAQs: - Can I connect multiple Google accounts? No — only 1 Google account per QP account. - Can multiple surveys connect to 1 sheet? Yes, but not recommended (interrupts simultaneous data flow). License availability: Essentials, Advanced, Team Edition, Research Edition QUALTRICS DATA IMPORT (QSF Migration Tool) What it does: Migrate surveys and responses from Qualtrics to QuestionPro using .qsf (survey) and .csv (response) files. How to export from Qualtrics: Survey → Tools → Import/Export → Export Survey → QSF file Responses → Data & Analysis → Export & Import → Export Data → CSV (use "choice text") How to import to QuestionPro: My Account → Integrations → Qualtrics Data Import → Upload QSF file Then: Import Data via CSV for responses Important warnings: - Do NOT edit the QSF file — it corrupts it - CSV file name must be 60 characters or less - Max CSV file size for response import: 20 MB Unsupported question types from Qualtrics: Highlight, Drill Down, Timing, Meta Info, Screen Capture, Side-by-Side Matrix, HeatMap, Hotspot, Card Sorting, Matrix Drop-down, Conjoint & MaxDiff Logic is NOT imported from QSF — must be re-applied manually in QuestionPro. License availability: Research Edition only API INTEGRATION What it is: QuestionPro uses a REST API with HTTP authentication. All responses are in JSON format. How to generate API key: Login → Surveys → Integration → API → Click "Get API Key" Note: API key is account-specific and unique per account. API Environments by Data Center: (Use {{env}} variable in API endpoint URLs based on your data center) - US: us - EU: eu - AE: ae - CA: ca - AU: au - KSA: ksa Help file: "Getting started with QuestionPro API" License availability: Available on all plans (check specific endpoint docs for plan restrictions)

body: QuestionPro — Internal Ticketing & Support Process Source: Internal QP Wiki (other-departments/customers/operations/ticketing-systems) TYPES OF TICKETS: 1. CLIENT ISSUE TICKET (Radar) Purpose: Track work done for the client — justifies subscription cost and provides transparency. Exposure: These tickets ARE visible to clients under "My Account" section. Use for: Any issue, feature gap, or task directly related to a client's experience. 2. INTERNAL INFORMATION TICKET (RE-Ticket) Purpose: Store and pass internal information between CSM and AM teams. Exposure: These are INTERNAL ONLY — not exposed to clients. Use for: Internal notes, crucial information that only the team needs. 3. BUG REPORTS Definition: When the product is not doing what it is advertised to do. Types: Feature issue, annoyance (too many clicks/too slow), UX issue, UI issue. Who reports: CSMs must report bugs of any magnitude to the product team. Timeline: Bugs requiring a build take at least 15 days to fix. How to report: Follow the "Report Bug" process document. 4. FEATURE REQUESTS Definition: Enhancements to existing functions/features or new capabilities. Process: 1. Raise on IdeaScale first 2. Get your team to upvote it 3. If business-critical, escalate to team leader Timeline: Feature requests can take 1 month to 1 year if accepted for development. DO NOT PROMISE features to clients without a YouTrack (YT) ticket AND PDM confirmation. TICKETING TOOL: Radar (admin.questionpro.com) DEVELOPMENT TRACKING: YouTrack (internal) PRODUCT IDEAS: IdeaScale

body: AI Router AI Router The AI Router An internal step-by-step guide for turning survey responses into structured AI analysis using AI Router, webhooks, JSON mapping, and custom variables. This cookbook is meant to be followed like a recipe. The goal is not just to explain what the AI Router can do, but to show exactly how to set it up inside a real survey workflow so another team member can reproduce it without guesswork. You can use this pattern to pull contextual intelligence from survey responses at the respondent level. That intelligence can then be saved back into the platform as structured data and reused in dashboards, BI, filters, logic, routing, journey maps, exports, and closed-loop workflows. At a high level, the setup works like this: a response comes into the survey, a webhook sends the relevant data to AI Router, AI Router processes that data using a prompt, the output comes back as JSON, that JSON is stored in a custom variable, and the values inside that JSON are then split into separate custom variables for reporting and downstream actions. Important: This workflow is highly dependent on prompt quality. If the prompt is vague, the output will be vague. If the prompt is inconsistent, the reporting layer will be inconsistent too. Treat the prompt as the logic layer of the whole setup. What can this setup be used for? Below are examples of analyses that can be generated from survey responses using this pattern. These are not limitations of the router. They are examples of what has already been tested or what fits naturally into the same setup. Sentiment analysis. Classify open text into Positive, Neutral, or Negative and optionally return sentiment tags, intensity, and confidence. This is useful when you want a dashboard-friendly field rather than reading each comment manually. Topics and themes. Return a primary topic and secondary topic, or a broader dominant theme and supporting theme. This is helpful when a single response may mention more than one issue, such as service quality, delivery delays, or billing confusion. Data legitimacy. Flag gibberish, spam, copied filler text, or responses that are too vague to analyze properly. This is useful for cleaning data before you build insights on top of it. NPS versus sentiment correlation. Compare the score and the comment together. For example, identify whether a low score aligns with negative language, or whether a high score contains hidden dissatisfaction. This gives you a stronger quality check than reading score or text alone. Insights and action items. Move beyond classification and have the router summarize what the feedback implies and what should be done next. This can be turned into operational recommendations, action logs, or management summaries. Improvement signals. Detect what the customer is asking to be changed and whether the signal is mild, moderate, or urgent. This is useful for priority-based follow-up. Journey stage mapping. Map a comment or a survey response to a st

summary: SLA Policies, Condition rules & SLA Status Source: https://www.questionpro.com/help/customer-experience/sla-policy.html Product: CX / Enhanced Closed Loop Category: Settings What are SLA Policies? SLA (Service Level Agreement) Policies allow Workspace Admins to define response and resolution targets
body: SLA Policies, Condition rules & SLA Status Source: https://www.questionpro.com/help/customer-experience/sla-policy.html Product: CX / Enhanced Closed Loop Category: Settings What are SLA Policies? SLA (Service Level Agreement) Policies allow Workspace Admins to define response and resolution targets for support tickets. Each policy contains Action SLA and Resolution SLA targets for every ticket priority, ensuring that tickets are handled within expected service timelines. SLA Policies work together with Business Hours and Holiday Calendar settings to calculate ticket deadlines accurately. Policies are automatically assigned to tickets using Condition rules or, if no rule matches, by the workspace's Default SLA Policy. Who can configure SLA Policies? Only Workspace Admins can manage SLA Policies and Condition rules. - Workspace Owner (System Administrator) - Shared users (Sub Accounts) with administrative access to the workspace Where can I configure SLA Policies? Navigate to: Closedloop → Workspace → Workspace Settings → SLA Policy What is the Default SLA Policy? Every workspace contains one Default SLA Policy that is automatically created by the system. - Only one Default SLA Policy exists per workspace. - The Default SLA Policy cannot be deleted. - The Default SLA Policy can be edited. - The Default SLA Policy does not support Condition rules. - The policy is applied automatically when no Condition rule matches a ticket. How do I create an SLA Policy? 1. Navigate to Closedloop → Workspace → Workspace Settings → SLA Policy. 2. Click Create Policy. 3. Enter a unique Policy Name. 4. Configure one or more Condition rules using the available ticket attributes. 5. Configure the Action SLA and Resolution SLA for each ticket priority. 6. Click Save to create the policy. How do Condition rules work? Condition rules determine which SLA Policy should be applied when a ticket is created. - Rules can evaluate Survey, Current Segment and Custom Variable fields. - You can configure up to five conditions within a policy. - Conditions can be combined using either AND or OR. - The system evaluates policies in the configured order and assigns the first matching policy. - If no rule matches, the Default SLA Policy is applied automatically. How do I configure SLA targets? Each SLA Policy contains separate Action SLA and Resolution SLA targets for every ticket priority: - Critical - High - Medium - Low Resolution SLA values must always be greater than the corresponding Action SLA values. How do I reorder SLA Policies? Non-default SLA Policies can be reordered to change the order in which Condition rules are evaluated. 1. Open the SLA Policy page. 2. Open the Reorder Policies dialog. 3. Drag and drop policies into the required order. 4. Save your changes. The Default SLA Policy always remains at the bottom of the list. Where can I view SLA information on a ticket? Open a ticket to view its SLA information. The SLA section on the Ticket Details page displays: - Action

body: Purpose Mindset Cookbook Purpose Mindset Purpose Mindset Cookbook Purpose Mindset: From Survey Inputs to Personal Purpose Statements Field Details Client / Initiative Purpose Mindset Initiative Led by Akhtar Badshah + QuestionPro Prepared for QuestionPro RE | Internal Use Primary platform QuestionPro Surveys, Communities, and AI Router | Document purpose | | | | This cookbook documents the complete Purpose Mindset implementation: survey setup, Community mapping, JavaScript logic, AI Router webhook configuration, output display, report download logic, and pricing model. | 1. Client and Problem Client Purpose Mindset Initiative — Akhtar Badshah + QuestionPro. Platforms Used · QuestionPro Surveys · QuestionPro Communities · AI Router using the OpenAI integration Background The Purpose Mindset exercise is designed to help individuals articulate their personal purpose by reflecting on two core areas: core strengths and foundational values. The experience needed to feel guided, reflective, premium, and personalized. The end goal was not just to collect survey responses, but to help each participant walk away with a generated personal purpose statement and a clear pathway for further engagement through a community and workshops. Specific Use Case The team wanted to create a simple but powerful survey-led flow where users could: 1. Select their top 3 strengths from a predefined list. 2. Select their top 3 values from a predefined list. 3. Receive a generated personal purpose statement. 4. Be classified as either Proactive or Responsive. 5. Be invited into a QuestionPro Community and future workshops. Key Requirements · Work inside the QuestionPro survey flow. · Generate the output in real time. · Allow the prompt to be edited without engineering dependency. · Deliver a premium and guided experience for the participant. · Integrate with QuestionPro Community for panel recruitment and continued engagement. 2. Solution Overview The solution combines QuestionPro Surveys, Communities, custom JavaScript, and AI Router. At a high level, the flow works as follows: 1. A QuestionPro Community is created for the Purpose Mindset initiative. 2. The survey collects participant profile information, strengths, and values. 3. Selected strengths and values are stored in custom variables. 4. A hidden survey question triggers a webhook to AI Router. 5. AI Router generates a purpose statement and classifies the participant as Proactive or Responsive. 6. The generated output is saved back into custom variables. 7. A final presentation/report screen displays the personalized purpose statement, purpose driver, golden nugget, strengths, and values. 8. The participant can download the report as an image. 9. The participant can be moved into the Purpose Mindset Community and invited to workshops. 3. Implementation Steps Step 1: Create the Community Create a dedicated QuestionPro Community for the Purpose Mindset initiative. Community URL: https://purpose.questionpro.com Community

Each client had a different context, but the same fundamental gap: the survey was a data collection form, not a conversation. It treated every respondent identically regardless of their score. It asked the same question whether the customer gave a 2 or a 9. It did nothing with the emotional signal embedded in the rating before asking the next question. body: The Dashboard Chef CUSTOM CX DASHBOARD WIDGETS Cookbook Turning QuestionPro's native CX dashboards into interactive, insight-driven experiences — built for Qatar Rail and Miral Destinations. Step-by-step: Source Widget → Custom HTML Widget → Filter Sync → AI Insights QuestionPro CX │ Internal Use 1. The Client & The Problem Background Qatar Rail and Miral Destinations both run active CX programmes on QuestionPro, collecting post-experience feedback across customer touchpoints. Their teams log into the QuestionPro dashboard regularly to review scores, track trends, and share insights across the organisation. The platform gives them the data — but not in a form that works for every audience. Executives need a single number and a clear signal. CX managers need trends and segment breakdowns. Analysts need to drill into distributions and drivers. The out-of-the-box QuestionPro dashboard serves all of them with the same layout: raw gauges, static bar charts, and no interactivity. Filters update the numbers, but they don't explain what changed or what to do about it. The Specific Gaps Dashboard widgets were designed for generic use, not the specific survey structure or KPI definitions each organisation cared about. Non-analyst stakeholders — executives and operations leads — saw charts, not decisions. There was no plain-English signal explaining what the data meant. No interactivity: a manager could not drill into a segment, compare periods, or pivot between views without leaving the dashboard entirely. Source widgets that existed only to feed data showed as blank white boxes on screen, making the dashboard look unfinished. After filters were applied, the dashboard updated silently with no indication of what changed or whether the numbers were meaningfully different. What Success Looked Like A CX manager opens the dashboard and immediately sees: the current CSAT score with a plain-English status (On track / Near benchmark), a CX Health Score combining CSAT, effort, and resolution data, and an anomaly banner if anything has moved significantly. She applies a filter for a specific support channel. Every widget updates simultaneously. She clicks a year bar in the effort tracker and the quarterly breakdown expands inline. She clicks the AI Insights widget and reads three prioritised actions generated from the live data. The entire experience happens without leaving the dashboard or opening a spreadsheet. 2. The Solution: Custom JavaScript Widgets on QuestionPro CX The solution uses QuestionPro's built-in Custom Widget (HTML widget) to inject JavaScript directly into dashboard tiles. Each custom widget reads live data from the native QuestionPro source widget sitting on the same dashboard, renders a bespoke UI, and re-renders automatically whenever a dashboard filter is applied. No external platform. No separate tool. No data exports. Everything runs inside the QuestionPro dashboard the teams already use. 3. Prerequisites QuestionPro Requirements Source Widg

summary: Behaviour on Different Sites Intercept widget deployment varies between traditional HTML sites and Single Page Applications (SPAs). Traditional HTML Websites: Each page load is a full server request and DOM reload. The JavaScript snippet must be included in a shared template (header/footer) to ensur
body: Behaviour on Different Sites Intercept widget deployment varies between traditional HTML sites and Single Page Applications (SPAs). Traditional HTML Websites: Each page load is a full server request and DOM reload. The JavaScript snippet must be included in a shared template (header/footer) to ensure it runs on every physical page load, as it is destroyed when the page unloads. Single Page Applications (SPAs): A single HTML file loads once. Subsequent navigation is handled by JavaScript ("soft navigation") without a full page reload, so the snippet, injected once in the main index.html, persists across the entire session. The challenge is ensuring the widget re-evaluates or re-triggers on these soft route changes, often requiring custom events or integration with the SPA's routing lifecycle.

This is a crucial rule for capturing immediate, in-the-moment feedback tied to a user's critical action (e.g., submitting a form, clicking
body: Button Rule The Button rule helps to show an intercept (like a survey or message) upon the click of a specific HTML button or link, identified by its unique id. This is a crucial rule for capturing immediate, in-the-moment feedback tied to a user's critical action (e.g., submitting a form, clicking "Buy Now," or unsubscribing). Example Scenario Imagine you want to show a short survey immediately after a customer clicks a "Download Report" button, whose ID in your website's code is download-report-button. How to Configure the Rule To check for a button click, you need to isolate and use the button's unique id attribute from your website's code. 1. Identify the Element: Use your browser's developer tools (Right-Click -> Inspect) to find the button or link you want to target. 2. Find the Key-Value pair: You will find the unique ID in the element's HTML tag, e.g., <button id="download-report-button"> Download Report </button>. 3. Select the Key: The key is the ID itself. The Rule Setup: * Rule: Button * Operand: Equals * Id: download-report-button Screenshots Flow : Demo Site Intercept

summary: Data Layer Rule The Data Layer is a way for your website to store information in the background (like customer type, product details, or page categories). Our intercept tool can "read" this information to decide when to show a survey. To set up a rule based on this data, you need to tell the system
body: Data Layer Rule The Data Layer is a way for your website to store information in the background (like customer type, product details, or page categories). Our intercept tool can "read" this information to decide when to show a survey. To set up a rule based on this data, you need to tell the system exactly where to look. We do this using a "Key" that points to the specific piece of information you want. Example Scenario Imagine your website has the following code running in the background. It defines a data layer with some information about an animal: ``` <script> var dataLayer = { animal : { name : "Lion", }, seg: "data_", } </script> ``` In this example, we want to target the name of the animal, which is "Lion". How to Configure the Rule To check if the animal name is "Lion", you need to provide the path to that specific value. 1. Identify the starting point: The main container is called dataLayer. 2. Find the category: Inside that, there is a category called animal. 3. Find the specific item: Inside animal, there is the specific item name The Rule Key: You combine these steps with dots (.) to create the key: dataLayer.animal.name The Rule Setup: * Key: dataLayer.animal.name * Operand: Equals (or Contains) * Value: Lion Screenshots Flow : Demo Site Intercept Result: When a visitor comes to your site, the intercept will look at dataLayer, find animal, then find name. If it finds the word "Lion", the rule is a match Limitations: We do not support dynamic keys. The keys must be exact to retrieve the required data, even if the element's position (e.g., its index in a window object) changes from one instance (like the 1st index) to another (like the 5th index).

summary: HTML Selector Rule The HTML Selector is a powerful targeting rule that allows you to display an intercept (like a survey or a feedback button) based on the content of your web page. It works by checking for the presence, absence, or content of specific HTML elements on the page a visitor is currentl
body: HTML Selector Rule The HTML Selector is a powerful targeting rule that allows you to display an intercept (like a survey or a feedback button) based on the content of your web page. It works by checking for the presence, absence, or content of specific HTML elements on the page a visitor is currently viewing. Think of it as a way to target users based on what they are seeing and interacting with on your site, even if that information isn't in the URL. How to set up Setting up the rule involves two main steps: Finding the Selector and Choosing the Operand.Step A: Finding the CSS Selector (The Element's Address) The CSS Selector is the unique "address" you use to tell the rule exactly which element to look for. You do not need to know how to code. Your Goal: Find the element's unique ID or Class in your browser's developer tools. 1. Go to the Page: Visit the exact web page where you want the rule to apply. 2. Right-Click & Inspect: Find the element you want to target (e.g., a "Buy Now" button). Right-click on it and select "Inspect." 3. Find the Code: A window (Developer Tools) will open, highlighting the element's code. If you see... Use this Selector Reliability id="submit-button" The ID with a hash sign: \#submit-button Best: IDs are unique and most reliable. class="primary-action" The Class with a period sign: .primary-action Good: Use a descriptive class name. Example: If the code is <button id="buy-now-btn" class="product-purchase-button">, the best selector is \#buy-now-btn.Step B: Configuring the Operand (The Rule's Action) Verify: Check if the element is accessible in the developers console. Example: ``` document.querySelectorAll("#submit-button").textContains ``` Note : A text value is required for the rule to match; it cannot return undefined. Best Practices & Tips * Use IDs Whenever Possible: An id is meant to be unique on a page, making it the most reliable selector. * Be Specific with Classes: Avoid generic class names like .button or .row. Look for more descriptive classes that are less likely to be used elsewhere. * Test Your Selector: Before saving your rule, you can test your selector in the browser's developer console. Press F12, go to the "Console" tab, type document.querySelector("your-selector-here"), and press Enter. If it returns the HTML element, your selector is correct! * Website Changes: If your website's design or code is updated, your selectors might break. If a rule suddenly stops working, the first thing to check is if the selector is still valid.

title: Intercept - Intercept Types

summary: Intercept Types Intercept Types Showcase: Maximizing UX for Feedback Collection This document provides a template for showcasing the three primary Intercept types, focusing on their unique characteristics, optimal use cases, and how each ensures a positive User Experience (UX). The Intercept decisio
body: Intercept Types Intercept Types Showcase: Maximizing UX for Feedback Collection This document provides a template for showcasing the three primary Intercept types, focusing on their unique characteristics, optimal use cases, and how each ensures a positive User Experience (UX). The Intercept decision engine determines the perfect moment to engage a visitor, moving from disruptive "Broadcasting" to timely "Conversing." 1. Feedback Popup (The Conversational Tap) The Feedback Popup is a highly controlled, non-intrusive modal that appears within the context of the page. It is designed for specific, in-the-moment feedback tied to an action or time spent on the page. Key UX Characteristic: Contextual and Low-Interruption * Behavior: Appears after a rule is met (e.g., 60 seconds on a page, 3 pages visited). * Design: Typically small or center-aligned, allowing the user to still view the page content behind it. * Goal: Capture feedback while the memory of the experience is fresh, minimizing disruption to the user's task flow. Use Case Trigger Rules (Example) Description Product Detail Review Time Spent (On Page) > 30 seconds and Page URL Includes /product-details/ Asks the user if they found the information they needed right when they are most engaged with the product. Post-Checkout Rating Page URL Equals thank-you.html and User Type Equals Returning Visitor Collects a quick satisfaction score immediately after a completed transaction. 2. Exit Intercept (The Last-Chance Rescue) The Exit Intercept triggers only when the user's mouse movement suggests they are about to leave the website (mouse movement tracked towards the browser's top bar). It is the final opportunity to engage a disengaged visitor. Key UX Characteristic: Non-Disruptive to Active Browsing * Behavior: Only triggers upon an "Exit Intent" action, ensuring it never interrupts active browsing. * Design: Can be a full-screen overlay to draw maximum attention to the rescue offer or question. * Goal: Recover an abandoned shopping cart, capture the reason for leaving, or offer a final incentive (like a discount). Use Case Trigger Rules (Example) Description Cart Abandonment Rescue Exit Intent Exists and Data Layer Includes cart_value > 0 Offers a discount code or free shipping to a customer who is about to leave with items still in their cart. Reason for Leaving Exit Intent Exists and Page URL Includes /home Asks a simple, one-question survey about why they are leaving the site so quickly. 3. Feedback Button (The Passive, Always-Available Option) The Feedback Button (often a small tab or icon anchored to the side of the screen) is a passive intercept. It remains visible on the page, allowing the user to initiate the survey or feedback form entirely on their own schedule. Key UX Characteristic: User-Initiated and Passive * Behavior: The button loads upon page initialization and remains available. The survey only appears upon the user's click. * Design: Minimalist design, usually a non-intrusive tab

summary: Operands We support the following operands for matching the assigned rules. Note that not all operands are utilized for every rule type. Operand What it does Equals to Exact Match. The data must be exactly identical to your value, character for character. Not Equals To Exclusion. Triggers for anythi
body: Operands We support the following operands for matching the assigned rules. Note that not all operands are utilized for every rule type. Operand What it does Equals to Exact Match. The data must be exactly identical to your value, character for character. Not Equals To Exclusion. Triggers for anything except the value you provide. Includes Partial Match. Looks for a specific word or phrase anywhere inside the data. Excludes Exclude Phrase. Triggers only if the specific word is missing. Starts With Prefix. Checks only the beginning of the data. Ends With Suffix. Checks only the very end of the data. Greater than More than. Checks if a number is larger than the value. Less Than Fewer than. Checks if a number is smaller than the value. Between Range. Checks if a number falls between two specific values. Exists Data is Present. Checks if the specific data point exists at all (is not empty).

summary: Setup Steps Intercept Snippet: Implementation Guide & Best Practices This document provides a clear, step-by-step guide on how to integrate the Intercept JavaScript snippet into your website. Proper implementation is crucial for ensuring the Intercept engine can correctly track user behavior, evalua
body: Setup Steps Intercept Snippet: Implementation Guide & Best Practices This document provides a clear, step-by-step guide on how to integrate the Intercept JavaScript snippet into your website. Proper implementation is crucial for ensuring the Intercept engine can correctly track user behavior, evaluate rules, and display surveys at the optimal moment. 1. Where to Place the Code Snippet The JavaScript snippet must be placed in a location where it executes on every single page load of your website. Site Type Placement Location Rationale Traditional HTML Site Within the <head> section of your global template (header/footer). Ensures the script is loaded and initialized before the page content to track early user behavior (like the first page URL and session start). Single Page Application (SPA) In the main index.html file, inside the <head> section. The script persists across all soft navigations (internal route changes) Best Practice: Always place the script as high as possible within the <head> tag. 2. How to Implement the Snippet (Step-by-Step) The following steps explain how to copy the unique snippet provided for your Intercept and paste it correctly. Step 1: Copy the Snippet 1. Navigate to the Intercept Setup tab. 2. Click the Copy button to get the entire code block. Standard Snippet Example (Use your unique API key) ``` <! Start of async Intercept tracking code > <script type="text/javascript" src="https://intercept-widget.questionpro.com/static/js/loader.js"></script> <script type="text/javascript"> var Intercept = { apiKey: [YOUR_API_KEY] }; </script> <! End of async Intercept tracking code > ``` Step 2: Access Your Website's Code You will need access to your website's main template files. This is usually done via your Content Management System (CMS), e-commerce platform's theme editor, or direct access to your site files. Platform Example How to Access the Code Shopify Online Store > Themes > Actions > Edit Code > theme.liquid WordPress Appearance > Theme File Editor > header.php Custom Site Access the shared template file that contains the opening <head> tag. Step 3: Paste the Code 1. Open the correct template file for editing. 2. Locate the closing </head> tag. 3. Paste the Intercept snippet immediately before the </head> tag. ``` <! Start of async Intercept tracking code > <script type="text/javascript" src="https://intercept-widget.questionpro.com/static/js/loader.js"></script> <script type="text/javascript"> var Intercept = { apiKey: [YOUR_API_KEY] }; </script> <! End of async Intercept tracking code > </head> <! The snippet is placed immediately above this tag > ``` Step 4: Save and Verify 1. Save the changes to your website's template file. 2. Open your website in a new browser tab. 3. Press F12 to open Developer Tools, and check the Console tab. You should see a message confirming the Intercept Engine has initialized. Special Considerations for Next.js (Client-Side Navigation) For Next.js, traditional <script> tags can fail on inte

summary: Targeting Rules Rules type for website (Engine) Country * Understanding: Target or exclude visitors based on their physical location. * Example Scenario: Show a survey only to visitors in Canada. * How to Configure: * Operand: Equals * Value: Select Canada from the list. * Result: Only visitors acce
body: Targeting Rules Rules type for website (Engine) Country * Understanding: Target or exclude visitors based on their physical location. * Example Scenario: Show a survey only to visitors in Canada. * How to Configure: * Operand: Equals * Value: Select Canada from the list. * Result: Only visitors accessing the site from a Canadian IP address will see the intercept. Browser Type * Understanding: Target visitors based on the web browser they are using (Chrome, Safari, Firefox, etc.). * Example Scenario: Test a new feature that only works on Google Chrome. * How to Configure: * Operand: Equals * Value: Select Chrome. * Result: Visitors using Safari or Edge will not see the survey. Device Type * Understanding: Distinguish between visitors on Desktop, Mobile, or Tablet. * Example Scenario: Show a shorter, mobile-friendly survey to phone users. * How to Configure: * Operand: Equals * Value: Select Mobile. * Result: Desktop users are ignored; only smartphone users see the intercept. OS (Operating System) * Understanding: Target the underlying system of the device (Windows, macOS, iOS, Android). * Example Scenario: Ask for feedback on your new iPhone App. * How to Configure: * Operand: Equals * Value: Select iOS. * Result: Only users on Apple mobile devices will match. User Type * Understanding: Differentiates between someone visiting for the first time versus someone coming back. * Example Scenario: Offer a "Welcome" discount only to new people. * How to Configure: * Operand: Equals * Value: Select New Visitor. * Result: If the system recognizes the user's cookie from a previous visit, the rule fails. IP Address * Understanding: Target a specific network address, often used for internal testing. * Example Scenario: Test the survey live, but only for people in your office. * How to Configure: * Operand: Equals * Value: 192.168.1.50 (Your specific IP). * Result: The general public cannot see the survey; only your specific network matches. Page URL * Understanding: Checks the specific web address the visitor is currently looking at. * Example Scenario: Show a survey only on the "Thank You" page after a purchase. * How to Configure: * Key: (Not needed, automatic) * Operand: Contains * Value: thank-you * Result: Matches www.site.com/checkout/thank-you but ignores www.site.com/home. Time Spent (On Page) * Understanding: Checks how long the user has been looking at the current page. * Example Scenario: Offer help only if a user has been stuck on the checkout page for more than 60 seconds. * How to Configure: * Operand: Greater Than * Value: 60 (Seconds) * Result: The survey waits 60 seconds before appearing. Session Duration (Total Time) * Understanding: Checks the total time the user has been on your website across all pages combined. * Example Scenario: Target highly engaged users who have been browsing your site for over 5 minutes. * How to Configure: * Operand: Greater Than * Value: 300 (Seconds) * Result: Triggers only after 5 minutes of total browsing. This metric counts multiple user visits within a single session, which is tied to the use of cookies. Limitations: The rule will only fu
body: Total Visits Rule This rule allows the intercept to be triggered when a user revisits the site, either by reloading the page or navigating from an external source. This metric counts multiple user visits within a single session, which is tied to the use of cookies. Limitations: The rule will only function if an intercept script is globally implemented within the application. For Single Page Applications (SPAs), this presents no issue as all scripts are placed in a single location. However, for traditional HTML sites where page navigation involves a full reload, all APIs and related resources are reloaded, which consequently increases the count.

summary: URL Parameter Rule URL Parameters (also known as Query Strings) are specific codes added to the end of your website link, usually after a question mark (?). They are commonly used in marketing emails, ad campaigns, or referral links to track where a visitor came from. Our intercept tool can "read" t
body: URL Parameter Rule URL Parameters (also known as Query Strings) are specific codes added to the end of your website link, usually after a question mark (?). They are commonly used in marketing emails, ad campaigns, or referral links to track where a visitor came from. Our intercept tool can "read" these parameters to target specific visitors. To set up a rule based on this, you simply need to know the name of the parameter you want to track . Example Scenario Imagine you have sent out a marketing email. When a customer clicks the link in that email, they land on your site with a URL that looks like this: ``` https://www.myshop.com/home?campaign=summer_sale&source=newsletter ``` In this example, we want to target visitors who came specifically from the newsletter. How to Configure the Rule To check if the visitor came from the newsletter, you need to isolate that specific part of the URL. 1. Identify the Parameter: Look at the URL after the ?. 2. Find the Key-Value pair: You will see pairs separated by an equals sign (=). In our example, we have source=newsletter. 3. Select the Key: The left side of the equals sign is the key (source). The Rule Key: You use the parameter name exactly as it appears in the URL: source The Rule Setup: * Key: source * Operand: Equals * Value: newsletter Screenshots Flow : Demo Site Intercept Result: When a visitor arrives at your site, the intercept will check the address bar. If it finds the parameter source and sees that it equals newsletter, the rule is a match and the survey will trigger. Limitations: * Rule will be checked only when website is completely loaded

summary: What Is Intercept The Art of the Digital "Tap on the Shoulder" Intercept is the intelligence layer that sits between your website and your visitors. It is not just a tool for showing pop-ups; it is a decision engine that determines the perfect moment to engage with a user. Instead of showing a gener
body: What Is Intercept The Art of the Digital "Tap on the Shoulder" Intercept is the intelligence layer that sits between your website and your visitors. It is not just a tool for showing pop-ups; it is a decision engine that determines the perfect moment to engage with a user. Instead of showing a generic survey to every person who lands on your homepage (which can be annoying), Intercept watches, waits, and listens. It analyzes who the visitor is, where they came from, and what they are doing right now. What are we achieving? The goal of Intercept is to move from "Broadcasting" to "Conversing." * Relevance: Only ask questions that matter to that specific user. * Precision: Target users based on deep data (like their cart value or membership level). * Experience: Improve customer satisfaction by offering help or asking for feedback exactly when it makes sense—not when it interrupts. A Real-World Example: The "Frustrated Shopper" The Scenario: Imagine a customer named Sarah. She is browsing your online store on her iPhone. She has added items to her cart but has been stuck on the "Checkout Payment" page for over 2 minutes without buying anything. Without Intercept: Sarah gets frustrated, closes the tab, and you lose the sale. You never know why. With Intercept: You have set up a "Cart Rescue" rule. The system detects: 1. Device: Mobile (She might be having trouble typing). 2. Page: Checkout. 3. Time Spent: > 120 seconds (She is stuck). The Result: Intercept triggers a friendly, non-intrusive popup: 🛖 Overview

body: ﻿Setting up the Intercept Setting up the Intercept What is a Mobile Intercept? Mobile Intercept lets you create, manage, and trigger intercepts within your mobile apps using the Intercept platform in QuestionPro. Whether you’re looking to gather feedback, promote a new feature, or check user sentiment at key app moments, this feature makes it possible natively within your mobile environment. Demo video Intercept rules demonstration - Recording How to create a mobile Intercept? Please visit the help file https://www.questionpro.com/help/customer-experience/mobile-intercept.html Android Android To create an Intercept for your mobile app, you need to create a Mobile Intercept using your QuestionPro account. Requirement The minimum Android version required is 21. Also, the compile SDK version for Android is 34 (But you should generally compile with the latest version)  Install the QuestionPro SDK Via Gradle Integration from the Maven Repository. The SDK is available on the central Maven repository. Add it to your root settings.gradle dependencyResolutionManagement { repositoriesMode.set(RepositoriesMode.FAIL_ON_PROJECT_REPOS) repositories { google() mavenCentral() maven { url 'https://jitpack.io' }//This line in required } }  QuestionPro’s SDK is published using the GitHub repository. Add a reference to the latest version of QuestionPro SDK in your app's build.gradle dependencies { implementation 'com.github.surveyanalyticscorp:android-cx:2.2.6' implementation 'com.google.code.gson:gson:2.10.1'//If not already added }  Please check the latest version of QuestionPro SDK here. Modify Manifest You need to modify your app’s Manifest file and add a few permissions, if not already added, which are required for the SDK. <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.INTERNET"/> <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.ACCESS_NETWORK_STATE"/>  Add the API key in the meta-data tag inside the manifest file. <meta-data android:name="cx_manifest_api_key" android:value="your api key here"/>  Add the following activity to show the feedback screen <activity android:name="com.questionpro.cxlib.InteractionActivity" android:theme="@android:style/Theme.Translucent.NoTitleBar" android:configChanges="keyboardHidden" android:windowSoftInputMode="adjustPan"/>  How to get the API key? Select the project in which you have created the intercept. Then, from the left navigation, select the Code option and copy the API key. Helpfile Usage The SDK initialisation should be done in the Application class. summary: QP JAVASCRIPT LOGIC — FLEX MATRIX ENGINE Derived from live console inspection: HPE PES 2026 Final Production (Survey ID: 13364444) Script block 224 (57,223 chars) — Satisfaction Feedback page Last updated: June 2026 WHAT THIS FILE COVERS The Flex matrix is QP's most advanced question type. body: QP JAVASCRIPT LOGIC — FLEX MATRIX ENGINE Derived from live console inspection: HPE PES 2026 Final Production (Survey ID: 13364444) Script block 224 (57,223 chars) — Satisfaction Feedback page Last updated: June 2026 WHAT THIS FILE COVERS The Flex matrix is QP's most advanced question type. Rows are dynamically shown or hidden based on answers from earlier in the survey — no two respondents see the same matrix. This file covers: 1. What a Flex matrix is and its DOM structure 2. The $survey API — all confirmed methods 3. The full Flex matrix row-control engine (production-verified template) 4. Dropdown visibility inside Flex matrix rows 5. Question code → numeric ID variable map 6. DOM selectors cheatsheet 7. Mixing Flex matrix with API: write row selections back to CX contacts 8. Debugging console snippets SECTION 1 — FLEX MATRIX DOM STRUCTURE A Flex matrix renders as multiple grouped tables — one per category. Each group has a parent-table with one row per item the respondent selected. KEY DOM ELEMENTS: .flex-matrix-question Top-level wrapper class .QCOMP Wrapper div for a specific matrix group (class = question code) .flex-matrix-question .table-wrapper .parent-table Outer table holding all matrix rows for one group tbody tr[role='listitem'] Data rows (excludes header rows which have no role) tbody tr:not([role]) Header rows (column labels: 0 1 2 ... 10) .child-table.ratingScaleTable Inner table per data row (contains radio inputs + text col) .answer-container.flex-matrix-q Answer container wrapper #QuestionSection_156183455 Section div for the QCOMP matrix #V_QuestionSection_65 Validation wrapper (V_ prefix, sequential number) KEY VARIABLE TYPES: MAIN_QS — Array of question codes for the matrix groups (e.g. ['QCOMP','Q75B']) Each is a .className wrapping one product-category matrix table. DRIVER_QS — Array of question codes for the product-selection checkboxes (e.g. ['Q19B','Q20B']). Their selected options determine which rows show. OPTION_ROW_MAP — Core mapping: { driverCode: { optionIndex: matrixRowNumber } } When option N of driver question Q is checked → show row R in the matrix. SECTION 2 — THE $survey API (all confirmed methods, live console inspection) $survey.getSelectedOptionsIndex('Q20B') → KEY METHOD for Flex matrix. → Returns array of selected option indexes (1-based). e.g. [1, 3, 5] → Returns [] if nothing selected. → This is what the Flex matrix engine reads — NOT the DOM directly. $survey.getSelectedOptions('Q9') → Returns array of selected option objects: [{id, text, value}] $survey.getSelectedOption('Q9') → Returns first selected option object (single-select questions) $survey.getSelectedOptionIndex('Q9') → Returns index of first selected option (single-select, 0-based) $survey.getSelectedCount('Q9') → Returns count of checked options (multi-select) $survey.getQuestionByCode('QCOMP') → Returns question object by its code (code set in builder, not auto-generated) $survey.showQuestion(qID) → Shows a question by numeric ID. API-le

summary: QP JAVASCRIPT LOGIC — SPREADSHEET MATRIX ENGINE Derived from live survey inspection: HPE Distribution RFI (Survey ID: 13187649) Last updated: June 2026 WHAT THIS FILE COVERS Spreadsheet matrix questions in QP support custom JavaScript that fires when respondents type into cells. body: QP JAVASCRIPT LOGIC — SPREADSHEET MATRIX ENGINE Derived from live survey inspection: HPE Distribution RFI (Survey ID: 13187649) Last updated: June 2026 WHAT THIS FILE COVERS Spreadsheet matrix questions in QP support custom JavaScript that fires when respondents type into cells. This file documents 5 distinct patterns found in a real production survey, plus templates for mixing them with QP API calls. Pattern 1 — YoY% Auto-Calculator (Q27, Q43) Pattern 2 — Flex Row Trimmer + Submit Handler (Q46) Pattern 3 — Budget Allocation 100% Cap Table (Q47) Pattern 4 — Headcount % Sum Table with MutationObserver (Q65) Pattern 5 — Simple Row Trimmer (Q49) Bonus — Character Limit Progress Meter API Mix — Combining matrix data with QP Surveys + CX APIs HOW SPREADSHEET MATRIX JS WORKS In the QP survey builder, you can add custom JavaScript to a spreadsheet matrix question via the question's "JavaScript" settings panel. This JS fires when the page loads and can also attach event listeners to matrix cells. The matrix DOM structure: .QUESTION_CODE .matrix-spreadsheet-question .table-wrapper .parent-table → The root table. Every script targets this. Cells are labelled with col_N classes (global sequential index across all rows): - QP does NOT give cells meaningful class names by default. - The first step in almost every pattern is to label them yourself. Input fields inside cells use the class: .form-input PATTERN 1 — YoY% AUTO-CALCULATOR Source: Q27 (3 vendor columns) and Q43 (4 vendor columns), HPE Distribution RFI PURPOSE: Automatically calculates year-over-year percentage change between columns. When a respondent enters a value in the "current year" column, the YoY% column auto-fills. YoY% cells are locked readonly. Handles all edge cases including zero-base years. HOW IT WORKS: 1. Label every td in table body with col_1, col_2, col_3... (global index) 2. bindPercentChange({input, base, output}) binds a listener between 3 columns 3. On change: read input col (current year) + base col (prior year) → calculate YoY% 4. Write result to output col (readonly) 5. Manual locked columns (e.g. Totals) pre-filled with '-' YoY% FORMULA LOGIC: Standard: ((currentYear - priorYear) / priorYear) * 100 0 → X: X * 100 (e.g. 0 to 3 partners = 300%) X → 0: -priorYear * 100 (e.g. 3 to 0 = -300%) 0 → 0: 0 0 → negative: output cleared COLUMN STRUCTURE — Q27 (3 data columns: HIT, Aruba, Juniper): Row 1 = 2024 actuals (cols 1-7: 3 data + 3 YoY% + 1 locked total) Row 2 = 2025 projected (cols 8-14: YoY vs row 1) Row 3 = 2026 projected (cols 15-21: YoY vs row 2) Row 4 = 2027 projected (cols 22-28: YoY vs row 3) Mappings for Q27: Row 2: { input: 8, base: 1, output: 9 } // HIT { input: 10, base: 3, output: 11 } // Aruba { input: 12, base: 5, output: 13 } // Juniper Row 3: { input: 15, base: 8, output: 16 } { input: 17, base: 10, output: 18 } { input: 19, base: 12, output: 20 } Row 4: { input: 22, base: 15, output: 23 } { input: 24, base: 17, output: 25 } { input: 26, base: 19, output: 27 } L

summary: QP JAVASCRIPT LOGIC — STANDARD SHOW/HIDE ENGINE Derived from live console inspection: HPE PES 2026 Final Production (Survey ID: 13364444) Last updated: June 2026 WHAT THIS FILE COVERS This file documents the standard QP show/hide question logic engine — the most commonly used JS pattern in QP survey
body: QP JAVASCRIPT LOGIC — STANDARD SHOW/HIDE ENGINE Derived from live console inspection: HPE PES 2026 Final Production (Survey ID: 13364444) Last updated: June 2026 WHAT THIS FILE COVERS This file documents the standard QP show/hide question logic engine — the most commonly used JS pattern in QP surveys. It covers: 1. How QP generates and names show/hide functions 2. The runtime execution sequence 3. Single-condition, multi-OR, and AND condition templates 4. Change event binding pattern 5. Default page state (startBinding) 6. The preExecuteJavaScriptLogic / postExecuteJavaScriptLogic hooks 7. Ready-to-use templates for all common scenarios 8. How to read radio, checkbox, dropdown, and text answers 9. Combining with QP API calls to push/pull data dynamically 10. Debugging console snippets SECTION 1 — HOW QP GENERATES SHOW/HIDE FUNCTIONS When you create a show/hide logic rule in the QP survey builder, QP automatically injects two types of functions into the survey page as inline <script> blocks: source_[sourceQID]_[ruleID]_orderNum_[N]() → Evaluates one condition. Returns true or false. → One function per condition per rule. criteriaUsedOn_[targetQID]() → Called every time a source question changes. → Calls all relevant source_ functions, combines them with AND/OR, then calls showSection() or hideSection() on the target question's div. NAMING CONVENTION: Source question ID → the question whose answer is being checked Target question ID → the question being shown or hidden Rule ID → auto-generated numeric ID for the logic rule Order number → position of this condition within the rule QUESTION SECTION DIV: Every question lives in: <div id="QuestionSection_[questionID]"> This is the element passed to showSection() / hideSection(). INPUT NAMING: Radio/checkbox answer IDs: [answerID]ID (e.g. 836960282ID) Question input name: u_[questionID] (e.g. u_156183394) Open text input name: t_[answerID] (e.g. t_836960285) SECTION 2 — CORE API FUNCTIONS (confirmed via live console inspection) SHOW / HIDE: showSection(el, animate) → Shows a question section div. → el = document.getElementById('QuestionSection_[qID]') → animate: 1 = fade in, 0 = instant hideSection(el, animate) → Hides a question section div. showDiv(id) → Shows any div by ID string hideDiv(id) → Hides any div by ID string startBinding(show[], hide[]) → Sets initial visibility state on page load. → Called once inside $(document).ready() → show[] = array of elements visible by default → hide[] = array of elements hidden by default submitFormIfAllAreHiddenQuestion() → Auto-advances the page if all questions are hidden by logic. → Called at bottom of each logic script block automatically. → Can also be called manually from postExecuteJavaScriptLogic(). VALIDATION: qp_validateSurvey() → Main validation. Returns true if page is valid. validateEmail(value) → true if valid email validateField(el) → Validates a single field, shows inline error validateNumeric(el) → Validates numeric input (min/max/decimal) vali

summary: QuestionPro CX Platform - RFP & RFI Capability Reference Source: Compiled from QuestionPro CX Platform RFP responses Purpose: Reference answers for sales reps completing RFPs/RFIs Note: Client-specific details removed. Answers reflect QP capabilities. CAPABILITIES & ANSWERS Q: How does your solution
body: QuestionPro CX Platform - RFP & RFI Capability Reference Source: Compiled from QuestionPro CX Platform RFP responses Purpose: Reference answers for sales reps completing RFPs/RFIs Note: Client-specific details removed. Answers reflect QP capabilities. CAPABILITIES & ANSWERS Q: How does your solution integrate with existing applications and systems, such as contact center systems, Salesforce, Adobe, etc.? A: QuestionPro integrates with existing applications and systems through 400+ integrations. These include (CRM) tools like Salesforce, as well as customer engagement and BI platforms. The integrations enable native bidirectional connectivity, ensuring seamless data exchange between QuestionPro and the connected systems. QuestionPro supports integration with applications such as Salesforce for CRM, and collaborates with tools like Office 365 and Teams for enhanced productivity and collaboration. We integrate with Zapier, which expands its connectivity options to various third-party applications Q: How does your solution link feedback and operational/transactional data from any channel to provide a 360 view of the customer? A: The QuestionPro platform is able to import operational & transactional data from various sources into the system using manual, automated and API driven processes. This data is available within the same workspace along with the survey & sample data, allowing users to create dashboards and analytics that combine all of these elements. For example, we can analyze survey responses alongside CLV or Transaction Tiers to understand the link between customer spend and CX. Our offline analytics team can conduct advanced analytics to understand how customer satisfaction/NPS drives financial outcomes. Q: How does your solution capture both solicited and unsolicited customer feedback? A: Our platform captures both solicited and unsolicited customer feedback through a variety of methods. Solicited Feedback includes asking customers for their opinions through a multiple channels - email, SMS, IVR, kiosks, phone interviews, in-app feedback, WA surveys. These methods target specific touchpoints and are designed to gather qual & quant insights directly from customers. Unsolicited Feedback includes passive or indirect feedback obtained through digital intercepts, social media interactions, customer care exchanges, and social reviews. Q: Does your solution allow organizations to be able to perform cross-channel sampling natively? How easy is it to alter the cross-channel sampling rules based on business needs? A: Yes, we offer different ways to capture data, even offline, with the help of an app we provide (each key is priced separately). We can offer web intercepts, surveys sent through email, QR code surveys, SMS (each SMS would have an extra cost), and WhatsApp integration (added cost). Also, we offer a panel integration for collecting sample (added cost). Q: Can survey logic be based on survey questions, meta data, device type, language, an

body: ﻿Custom Integrations Middleware ________________ Middleware Playbook Latest update: July 17, 2025 Overview Pricing Minimum requirements for Middleware ARR Minimum commitment Charges Setup fee Change requests Server Charges Usecases: 1. ETL Integrations (Extract, Transform, Load) What it does: Common Scenarios: Key Features: 2. SMS Integrations Common Scenarios: 3. WhatsApp Integrations What it does: Common Scenarios: Internal Process for Raising a TechOps Request for Middleware Important note Implementation Process Flow What can be done Sample Scenarios Net new requirements Change requests/Feature Enhancements Implementations done for the clients Overview Middleware is designed to seamlessly integrate third-party systems with QuestionPro. This solution will act as a centralized hub that enables secure, scalable, and customizable data interactions between external systems and the QuestionPro platform. Pricing Minimum requirements for Middleware ARR $30K ARR Minimum commitment 3 years Charges Setup fee $15k on cost basis for ARR below $50K $30k on cost basis for ARR above $50K (For a deal of $60k, the charges for middleware will be $15k in that $60k) Change requests Between $1000 to $5000 [For any changes required after initial implementation] Server Charges As per scope of the project (Recurring annually) ________________ Usecases: At QuestionPro, middleware requests typically fall into four categories: ETL integrations for syncing data with external systems, SMS integrations for sending surveys or running conversational surveys, WhatsApp integrations for interactive surveys using CMILES, and change requests, often involving updates to logic or providers—most commonly, requests for log data dumps for audit or debugging. 1. ETL Integrations (Extract, Transform, Load) What it does: Facilitates the movement of data to and from QuestionPro, enabling customers to keep their internal systems in sync with survey data or to trigger surveys based on business events. Common Scenarios: * Exporting survey responses to CRMs (e.g., Salesforce, HubSpot) or data warehouses * Importing contact lists, transactions or metadata from internal databases into QuestionPro for targeted surveys. * Scheduled or event-driven sync jobs / Automated daily or weekly ETL pipelines. Key Features: * Custom data mapping and transformation * Scheduled or real-time pipelines * Logging, retries, and audit trails 2. SMS Integrations What it does: Enables survey distribution and conversational surveys via third-party SMS providers. Common Scenarios: * Sending transactional or post-interaction surveys via SMS. * Full conversational SMS surveys (one question per message). * Automating reminders and follow-ups via SMS. ________________ 3. WhatsApp Integrations What it does: Leverages WhatsApp Business APIs via CMILES to enable both survey invites and conversational surveys via the world's most used messaging app. Common Scenarios: * Trigger-based WhatsApp surveys (e.g., case closed, deliver

summary: Getting Started: API stands for "application programming interface." This Wikipedia article covers APIs in more detail, but it can be helpful to think of the API as a way for different apps to talk to
body: Getting Started Getting Started: API stands for "application programming interface." This Wikipedia article covers APIs in more detail, but it can be helpful to think of the API as a way for different apps to talk to one another. For many users, the main interaction with the API will be through API access key, a way for other apps to access your account without you giving out your password. The QuestionPro API is organized around REST. We use built-in HTTP features, like HTTP authentication and HTTP verbs, to allow you to interact securely with our API from a client-side web application. JSON will be returned in all responses from the API, including errors.
content:
Getting Started

Getting Started: API stands for "application programming interface." This Wikipedia article covers APIs in more detail, but it can be helpful to think of the API as a way for different apps to talk to one another. For many users, the main interaction with the API will be through API access key, a way for other apps to access your account without you giving out your password. The QuestionPro API is organized around REST. We use built-in HTTP features, like HTTP authentication and HTTP verbs, to allow you to interact securely with our API from a client-side web application. JSON will be returned in all responses from the API, including errors.

summary: Authentication API authentication is an essential part of obtaining access to QuestionPro's API services. To ensure the security and confidentiality of your data, we utilise the API key authentication
body: Authentication Authentication API authentication is an essential part of obtaining access to QuestionPro's API services. To ensure the security and confidentiality of your data, we utilise the API key authentication method. Your API key can be managed through your QuestionPro account. Read: How to generate API Key? In order to authenticate you can use the standard api-key parameter. It has to be passed as a header string parameter. Authentication: arrow_rightExample Request URL https://api.questionpro.{{env}}/a/api/v2/micropanel/members Header api-key : {{api-key}} Note: Please do not share your secret API keys in publicly accessible areas such GitHub, client-side code, and so forth.
content:
Authentication

Authentication API authentication is an essential part of obtaining access to QuestionPro's API services. To ensure the security and confidentiality of your data, we utilise the API key authentication method. Your API key can be managed through your QuestionPro account. Read: How to generate API Key? In order to authenticate you can use the standard api-key parameter. It has to be passed as a header string parameter. Authentication: arrow_rightExample Request URL https://api.questionpro.{{env}}/a/api/v2/micropanel/members Header api-key : {{api-key}} Note: Please do not share your secret API keys in publicly accessible areas such GitHub, client-side code, and so forth.

body: Environments Environments Environments: The environment for your API endpoint depends upon your account's data center. Please go through the following table to check the value of the enivronment variable {{env}} for your API endpoints: Datacenter Environment Value Example Endpoint US com https://api.questionpro.com/a/api/v2/micropanel/members EU eu https://api.questionpro.eu/a/api/v2/micropanel/members CA ca https://api.questionpro.ca/a/api/v2/micropanel/members AE ae https://api.questionpro.ae/a/api/v2/micropanel/members AU com.au https://api.questionpro.com.au/a/api/v2/micropanel/members Use the above environment value as per your datacenter to replace {{env}} in your API calls. If your account is on Surveyanalytics, US Government or AE Government then please check the following table to get the API endpoint URL for your datacenter: Datacenter Endpoint domain Example URL Surveyanalytics api.surveyanalytics.com https://api.surveyanalytics.com/a/api/v2/micropanel/members US Government api.questionprogov.com https://api.questionprogov.com/a/api/v2/micropanel/members How to find out the data center for your QuestionPro account? To view the data center for your account, please follow the steps below: Login to your QuestionPro account. Click on the My Account icon on the top-right corner. At the bottom of the My Account dropdown, you can see the datacenter for your QuestionPro account. Read the help file for more information

body: Send Batch Post /micropanel/batches Example Request https://api.questionpro.{{env}}/a/api/v2/micropanel/batches The request body requires template language ID and Survey Language ID as an input. So, in order to have that, please refer this Link The value of environment {{env}} variable depends upon your datacenter. Refer to the Environment page for more details. Authorization arrow_rightSecurity - API Key Name : api-key required Location : Request Header Type : string Some pointers In order to fetch the templateID, use this API The parameter filterType can contain any of the integer value from 1-3. Where 1- DEFAULT_FILTER_TYPE (survey invite will be sent to all verified members) Example: "recipients": { "filterType": 1 } 2- EMAIL_FILTER_TYPE (survey invite will be sent to only a particular member) Example: "recipients": { "filterType": 2, "emailAddress": "abc@test.com" } 3- EXISTING_FILTER_TYPE (survey invite will be sent to existing filter selected) Example: "recipients": { "filterType": 3, "existingFilterID" : 145 } In order to fetch the existingFilterID, please refer API Request Body arrow_rightPayload application/json { "compose": { "surveyID": 11372241, "surveyAlias": "", "surveyLength": 8, "surveyLanguageID": 486, "templateID": 118632, "templateLanguageID": 1, "allowMultipleResponses": true, "incentives": { "completionPoints": 20, "quotaPoints": 30, "terminationPoints": 40 } }, "recipients": { "filterType": 2, "emailAddress": "test@questionpro.com", }, "send": { "deliveryOptions": { "automaticClosure": { "enabled": true, "closureDateTime": "12/10/2023 13:43", "responseQuota": 10 } }, "notification": { "enableEmailInvitation": false } } } arrow_rightInput Schema surveyID integer It should be survey id which is accessible by panel owner templateID integer Refer Point 1 allowMultipleResponses boolean By default it will be false. filterType integer (1-3) Refer Point 2 closureDateTime timestamp MM/dd/yyyy HH:mm (24 hours format is used) enableEmailInvitation boolean By default it will be false.
