title: Add advanced reports to Dashboards
kw: report dashboard bi text add ai reports step crosstab data tab multiple one advanced identify select click dashboards unified cross view users added feature analytical widgets consolidated switch between tabs all insights save choose more section created want integrate analysis
summary: Learn how to use the Unified BI Dashboard Integration feature to consolidate BI, Cross Tab, and Text AI reports into a single view.
body: Add advanced reports to Dashboards Learn how to use the Unified BI Dashboard Integration feature to consolidate BI, Cross Tab, and Text AI reports into a single view. Add advanced report to BI dashboard Introduction The add report feature allows users to bring together multiple analytical reports, including BI widgets, Cross Tab reports, and Text AI reports — into a single, consolidated BI dashboard. With this feature, users no longer need to switch between multiple report tabs or interfaces. Instead, they can centralize all key insights in one place, ensuring a seamless and holistic view of their data. Purpose and Benefits Centralized Insight Hub – Combine data visualizations, cross tab analyses, and text AI summaries into one comprehensive dashboard. Improved Efficiency – Save time by viewing and comparing multiple report types without navigating across tabs. Customizable Dashboards – Users can choose which reports to add as widgets, tailoring dashboards to their unique analytical needs. Collaborative Reporting – Share one unified BI report instead of multiple separate ones, making data storytelling simpler and more effective. Step by step guide Step 1 Identify crosstab report: Navigate to the 'Reports' section. Here, you can view all existing reports created. Identify the crosstab report you’ve created to add it to your BI Dashboard. Step 2 Identify text AI report to add to BI report: Open the TextAI Dashboards section. Identify the Text AI report that you want to integrate with your main BI Dashboard for consolidated analysis. Step 3 Select BI dashboard: Identify the BI dashboard where you want to add your Crosstab or Text AI report. This determines where your consolidated report will appear. Step 4 Add report on BI dashboard: Once inside your BI Dashboard, click the “+” icon and select 'Report' to begin adding a crosstab or text AI report as a new tab or widget within the dashboard. Step 5 Select crosstab to be added to the BI report: In the New Report Tab window, choose the Crosstab section, select the report you want to add, and click Save. This will integrate your selected Crosstab report into the BI dashboard. Step 6 Crosstab report added to BI dashboard: Your selected crosstab report is now successfully added to the BI dashboard. You can apply additional filters or modify settings as needed to refine the displayed data. Step 7 Click report to add more advanced reports: To add more analytics, click the “+” icon again and choose Report. This allows you to integrate additional advanced reports such as Text AI into your BI dashboard. Step 8 Add text analytics to BI dashboard: In the new report tab window, switch to the Text AI tab, select the dashboard where your Text AI analysis was created, and click Save to add it to your BI dashboard. Step 9 Text AI report integrated to BI dashboard: The Text AI report is now integrated into your BI Dashboard alongside your crosstab report. Users can easily switch between tabs to view all insights—quant
content:
Add advanced reports to Dashboards

Learn how to use the Unified BI Dashboard Integration feature to consolidate BI, Cross Tab, and Text AI reports into a single view.

Add advanced report to BI dashboard Introduction The add report feature allows users to bring together multiple analytical reports, including BI widgets, Cross Tab reports, and Text AI reports — into a single, consolidated BI dashboard. With this feature, users no longer need to switch between multiple report tabs or interfaces. Instead, they can centralize all key insights in one place, ensuring a seamless and holistic view of their data. Purpose and Benefits Centralized Insight Hub – Combine data visualizations, cross tab analyses, and text AI summaries into one comprehensive dashboard. Improved Efficiency – Save time by viewing and comparing multiple report types without navigating across tabs. Customizable Dashboards – Users can choose which reports to add as widgets, tailoring dashboards to their unique analytical needs. Collaborative Reporting – Share one unified BI report instead of multiple separate ones, making data storytelling simpler and more effective. Step by step guide Step 1 Identify crosstab report: Navigate to the 'Reports' section. Here, you can view all existing reports created. Identify the crosstab report you’ve created to add it to your BI Dashboard. Step 2 Identify text AI report to add to BI report: Open the TextAI Dashboards section. Identify the Text AI report that you want to integrate with your main BI Dashboard for consolidated analysis. Step 3 Select BI dashboard: Identify the BI dashboard where you want to add your Crosstab or Text AI report. This determines where your consolidated report will appear. Step 4 Add report on BI dashboard: Once inside your BI Dashboard, click the “+” icon and select 'Report' to begin adding a crosstab or text AI report as a new tab or widget within the dashboard. Step 5 Select crosstab to be added to the BI report: In the New Report Tab window, choose the Crosstab section, select the report you want to add, and click Save. This will integrate your selected Crosstab report into the BI dashboard. Step 6 Crosstab report added to BI dashboard: Your selected crosstab report is now successfully added to the BI dashboard. You can apply additional filters or modify settings as needed to refine the displayed data. Step 7 Click report to add more advanced reports: To add more analytics, click the “+” icon again and choose Report. This allows you to integrate additional advanced reports such as Text AI into your BI dashboard. Step 8 Add text analytics to BI dashboard: In the new report tab window, switch to the Text AI tab, select the dashboard where your Text AI analysis was created, and click Save to add it to your BI dashboard. Step 9 Text AI report integrated to BI dashboard: The Text AI report is now integrated into your BI Dashboard alongside your crosstab report. Users can easily switch between tabs to view all insights—quantitative and qualitative—in one unified dashboard. Glossary Below are definitions of key terms used in this help file: BI Dashboard – A centralized workspace for visualizing multiple data sources and reports in one place. Widget – A modular visual component (e.g., chart, table, text summary) added to the BI Dashboard for analysis. Cross Tab Report – A report that displays data in a matrix format to analyze relationships between variables. Text AI Report – A report powered by AI that analyzes open-ended responses or textual data for themes, sentiment, and insights. Unified Dashboard Integration – The process of bringing multiple analytical report types together within one BI Dashboard. Data Synchronization – Automatic updating of dashboard widgets when underlying report data is modified. Was the article helpful? Yes No How can we improve it? summary: Understanding and Using Histograms in Data Visualization
body: Histogram Understanding and Using Histograms in Data Visualization Histogram Introduction Histogram is a powerful data visualization tool used to represent the distribution of numerical data. It works by grouping data values into intervals, or "bins", and then displaying the frequency of occurrences within each bin using vertical bars. The height of each bar corresponds to the number of data points that fall within its associated bin. Histograms are particularly valuable for understanding the shape, center, and spread of data. They can reveal patterns, such as symmetry, skewness, and the presence of outliers. Additionally, histograms help identify the mode and provide insights into the range and variability of the data. In survey data, histograms can visualize responses to numerical questions like age, satisfaction ratings, or income levels, helping researchers understand the sample's characteristics. What are the purposes and benefits of histograms? Visual representation of data distribution Identification of patterns and trends Estimation of central tendency and dispersion Comparison of data sets Detection of outliers Getting Started Coming Soon> Steps for setting up histograms BI -> Create Dashboard BI -> Create Dashboard -> Blank Dashboard -> Continue BI -> Create Dashboard -> Blank Dashboard -> Continue -> Add Widget BI -> Create Dashboard -> Blank Dashboard -> Continue -> Add Widget -> Question Based BI -> Create Dashboard -> Blank Dashboard -> Continue -> Add Widget -> Question Based -> Select Survey BI -> Create Dashboard -> Blank Dashboard -> Continue -> Add Widget -> Question Based -> Select Survey -> Select Numeric Question BI -> Create Dashboard -> Blank Dashboard -> Continue -> Add Widget -> Question Based -> Select Survey -> Select Numeric Question -> Select Numeric Histogram -> Add Widget BI -> 3 dots BI -> 3 dots -> Settings BI -> 3 dots -> Settings -> Analytics -> Custom BI -> 3 dots -> Settings -> Analytics -> Custom -> Change Bin size BI -> 3 dots -> Settings -> Analytics -> Custom -> Change Bin size -> Histogram widget What is the core functionality of histograms? Field Selector: Supports numeric data only, including integers. Bin Control: Manual Binning: Define custom bin ranges. Auto Binning: Automatically creates bins based on distribution and bin width. Bin Width: Sets the size of each bin (e.g., 5-unit intervals). Chart Display: Dynamic updates as settings change. Hover tooltips showing bin range and frequency. Customization Panel: Modify title, axis labels, number of bins, and color scheme. Glossary of histogram-related terms Bin: A range or interval grouping data points; forms the base of histogram bars. Choosing the right bin width balances detail with readability. Frequency: Count of data points within a bin; bar height in a histogram represents frequency. Cumulative Histogram: Displays cumulative frequencies, showing running totals and aiding percentile/quantile estimation. Normalized: Frequencies expressed as percen

In survey data, histograms can visualize responses to numerical questions like age, satisfaction ratings, or income levels, helping researchers understand the sample's characteristics. body: Gauge Understanding and Using the Gauge Feature for Key Metric Visualization Gauge Widget Introduction The Gauge chart is a powerful visualization tool designed to display and track essential metrics, such as Mean, NPS (Net Promoter Score), and CES (Customer Effort Score). It presents these metrics in a clear and concise gauge-style dial format, making it easy for users to quickly understand and interpret the data. This visual representation is particularly valuable for dashboards and executive reporting, where stakeholders need to grasp key performance indicators at a glance. By providing a visually engaging and informative representation of critical metrics, the Gauge feature enhances data-driven decision-making and facilitates effective communication of results. Purposes and Benefits of Gauge Widget Visualize essential scores in real-time using gauges. Add context to metrics through colors and thresholds. Improve readability of KPIs for quick decision-making. title: Scoring Donut

summary: Scoring Donut Help Documentation
body: Scoring Donut Scoring Donut Help Documentation Scoring Donut Widget Introduction The Scoring Donut is a sleek, intuitive data visualization component designed to showcase scoring metrics such as averages, satisfaction scores, or performance ratings in a circular, donut-style chart. It excels at showcasing scoring metrics like averages, satisfaction scores, and performance ratings. This makes it ideal for dashboards, reports, and presentations where conveying overall performance at a glance is crucial. The donut chart's key strength lies in its ability to represent numeric scores within a circular format. This allows for intuitive interpretation, as the viewer can quickly gauge the score's position relative to the full range of possible values. Additionally, the central hole can be utilized to display the actual score or other relevant information, further enhancing the chart's informational value. By leveraging the Scoring Donut's visual impact and clarity, users can effectively communicate complex scoring data to a wide audience, regardless of their technical expertise. This makes it a valuable tool for data-driven decision-making and performance tracking across various industries and applications. Purpose and Benefits Compact and Visually Appealing: Scoring Donut charts efficiently display critical metrics in a visually engaging and concise manner, making them easy to understand at a glance. Instant Clarity: The circular design provides immediate comprehension of scores and performance levels, enhancing the effectiveness of dashboards and reports. Versatility: Gauge charts can be customized to suit diverse applications, including: Survey Data Visualization: Displaying survey response scores, such as customer satisfaction ratings or employee engagement levels Performance Tracking: Visualizing team or individual performance metrics, such as sales targets or key performance indicators (KPIs) Customizability: Scoring Donut charts offer flexibility in terms of colors and labels, allowing for adaptation to specific requirements and branding guidelines. Core Functionality Score Representation Displays numeric scores (e.g., 75 out of 100) at the center of the donut. Supports multiple scoring formats: percentage, 5-point scale, 10-point scale, etc. Donut Visualization The colored arc around the center represents the score proportionally. Customizable colors based on thresholds or scoring tiers (e.g., green for high scores, red for low). Dynamic Updates Scores update in real time when underlying data changes. Ideal for interactive dashboards where user inputs or filters affect the displayed score. summary: Guide to using the Stacked Bar feature for visualizing Unichoice and Multichoice categorical data.
body: Stacked Bar Guide to using the Stacked Bar feature for visualizing Unichoice and Multichoice categorical data. Stacked Bar Introduction The Stacked Bar feature enables users to visualize category data using stack bar charts. This feature supports both Unichoice and Multichoice categorical variables, making it ideal for survey data where respondents may select one or multiple answers. Purpose and Benefits: Gain insights into the distribution and comparison of categorical data. Easily interpret proportions of responses within a group. Efficiently represent overlapping data segments in a single visual. Ideal for tracking multi-dimensional categorical responses over groups or time. Core Functionality The Stacked Bar feature is designed to help you create insightful and layered bar charts from categorical datasets. Supported Inputs Unichoice Category: A category where each respondent selects only one option. Multichoice Category: A category where each respondent can select multiple options. Getting Started Coming Soon Step by Step Guide BI -> Create Dashboard BI -> Create Dashboard -> Blank Dashboard -> Continue BI -> Create Dashboard -> Blank Dashboard -> Continue -> Question Based BI -> Create Dashboard -> Blank Dashboard -> Continue -> Question Based -> Select Survey BI -> Create Dashboard -> Blank Dashboard -> Continue -> Question Based -> Select Survey -> Select Survey Question BI -> Create Dashboard -> Blank Dashboard -> Continue -> Question Based -> Select Survey -> Select Survey Question -> Stack Bar Widget BI -> Stack Bar Widget -> 3 dots -> Settings BI -> Stack Bar Widget -> 3 dots -> Settings -> Analytics -> Chart Orientation, Data Ordering, Top Answer value to display BI -> Stack Bar Widget -> 3 dots -> Settings -> Analytics -> Widget Stats BI -> Stack Bar Widget Glossary Unichoice: A single-choice question or variable where users select only one response. Multichoice: A question or variable allowing multiple selections per respondent. Stack: Each segment of the bar representing a portion of the category data. Normalization: Adjusting values to a common scale, such as converting counts to percentages. Was the article helpful? Yes No How can we improve it? Submit Thank you for submitting feedback.

title: Tabular

summary: Tabular is a simplified table-based view for summarizing matrix data and comparing respondent groups based on qualification criteria.
body: Tabular Tabular is a simplified table-based view for summarizing matrix data and comparing respondent groups based on qualification criteria. Tabular Introduction Tabular is a powerful tabulation feature available on the BI platform. It enables users to view summarized data from matrices in a clean, table-based format. Designed for simplicity and efficiency, Tabular presents only the overall value for each metric, making it an ideal choice for high-level summaries and executive reporting. By focusing solely on the overall figures, Tabular ensures clarity, minimizes cognitive load, and facilitates quick data interpretation. Tabular also analyzes data for different groups of respondents based on specific qualification criteria from your survey. The feature is designed to help you slice your data and compare how different segments of respondents answered key questions. By segmenting your data—such as by age, region, or purchase behavior—you can see how each group’s responses compare to the overall sample. This helps you identify patterns, validate hypotheses, and generate deeper insights into respondent behavior. Core Functionality Summary-Only Display Tabular outputs provide a condensed version of your matrix by displaying only the overall value of each measure. It eliminates row and column breakdowns, which is useful when the focus is on total impact rather than segmented analysis. Simplified Visualization Since Tabular is optimized for clarity, it avoids complex visual elements. The result is a minimalist and structured table that prioritizes data readability. Seamless Integration Tabular can be easily applied to any matrix object within the BI platform. Users can toggle between visual and tabular formats depending on the reporting need. content:
Tabular

Tabular is a simplified table-based view for summarizing matrix data and comparing respondent groups based on qualification criteria.

Tabular Introduction Tabular is a powerful tabulation feature available on the BI platform. It enables users to view summarized data from matrices in a clean, table-based format. Designed for simplicity and efficiency, Tabular presents only the overall value for each metric, making it an ideal choice for high-level summaries and executive reporting. By focusing solely on the overall figures, Tabular ensures clarity, minimizes cognitive load, and facilitates quick data interpretation. Tabular also analyzes data for different groups of respondents based on specific qualification criteria from your survey. The feature is designed to help you slice your data and compare how different segments of respondents answered key questions. By segmenting your data—such as by age, region, or purchase behavior—you can see how each group’s responses compare to the overall sample. This helps you identify patterns, validate hypotheses, and generate deeper insights into respondent behavior. Core Functionality Summary-Only Display Tabular outputs provide a condensed version of your matrix by displaying only the overall value of each measure. It eliminates row and column breakdowns, which is useful when the focus is on total impact rather than segmented analysis. Simplified Visualization Since Tabular is optimized for clarity, it avoids complex visual elements. The result is a minimalist and structured table that prioritizes data readability. Seamless Integration Tabular can be easily applied to any matrix object within the BI platform. Users can toggle between visual and tabular formats depending on the reporting need. Step By Step Guide BI -> Create dashboard BI -> Create dashboard -> Blank Dashboard BI -> Create dashboard -> Blank Dashboard -> Add widget BI -> Create dashboard -> Blank Dashboard -> Add widget -> Question Based BI -> Create dashboard -> Blank Dashboard -> Add widget -> Question Based -> Select Survey BI -> Create dashboard -> Blank Dashboard -> Add widget -> Question Based -> Select Survey -> Select Question BI -> Create dashboard -> Blank Dashboard -> Add widget -> Question Based -> Select Survey -> Select Question -> Tabular Widget -> Add Widget BI -> Tabular Widget BI -> Dashboard -> Settings -> Data Slicer BI -> Dashboard -> Settings -> Data Slicer -> Data Criterion BI -> Dashboard -> Settings -> Data Slicer -> Data Criterion -> List of Data Slicer BI -> Tabular Widget -> 3 dots -> Settings BI -> Tabular Widget -> 3 dots -> Settings -> Analytics -> Data slicer options -> Widget -> Select data slicer BI -> Tabular Widget -> 3 dots -> Settings -> Analytics -> Data slicer options -> Widget -> Select data slicer -> Widget Stats BI -> Tabular Widget Glossary Tabular: A feature that displays summarized matrix data in a table format showing only the overall values. Matrix: A data structure that displays values in rows and columns, often used to show relationships between variables. Summarization: The process of aggregating data to show key metrics or totals. Qualification Criteria: Conditions based on survey variables used to define respondent groups (e.g., age ≥ 25). Frequency: The count of respondents who selected a particular response. Proportion: The percentage of a group (or total) that selected a particular response. Slice: To filter or segment the data based on a condition. Overall Sample: The full respondent base that completed the survey, used as a benchmark. Was the article helpful? Yes No How can we improve it? Submit Thank you for submitting feedback.

title: Matrix Stackbar

summary: Learn how to use Matrix Stackbar in the BI dashboard to visualize and compare matrix-style survey data using horizontal or vertical stacked bar charts.
body: Matrix Stackbar Learn how to use Matrix Stackbar in the BI dashboard to visualize and compare matrix-style survey data using horizontal or vertical stacked bar charts. Matrix Stackbar Introduction Matrix Stackbar is a powerful visualization feature within the BI dashboard that enables users to build horizontal or vertical stacked bar charts specifically for matrix-style survey questions. These questions typically involve multiple attributes or items being measured using the same response scale (e.g. Likert scale, awareness option, usage option, ownership option etc.). The feature provides a clear and comparative view of how different attributes are rated by respondents, helping users identify trends, strengths, and improvement areas across multiple items in one visual. Core Functionality Key Capabilities of the Matrix Stackbar Feature: Matrix-Based Visualization Matrix Stackbar works with questions where multiple attributes share the same response options. Each attribute appears as a separate bar, divided into segments that represent the distribution of responses across the common scale. Stacked Bar Layout (Horizontal or Vertical) Users can toggle between horizontal and vertical stacked bars depending on the desired layout for reporting or presentation. Each stack reflects how respondents distributed their answers for a particular attribute. Attribute Comparison The feature is ideal for comparing sentiments or ratings across several attributes—making it especially useful for brand perception, service evaluation, or product feature assessments. Consistent Scale Handling The chart automatically aligns and colors segments consistently across all bars, allowing quick interpretation of positive, neutral, or negative tendencies. Common Tasks: Compare satisfaction levels across different service attributes in a customer experience survey. Visualize how various brands are perceived on the same set of traits (e.g., affordability, quality, innovation). Analyze employee feedback across different departments or processes using a common feedback matrix. title: Spider Widget
kw: dashboard widget survey spider bi question across matrix chart create data scale blank multiple attributes same add select feature satisfaction based compare different sentiment waves response series type guide style questions brand perceptions traits trust value innovation track employee scores
summary: Overview and usage guide for the Spider Widget feature in the BI dashboard for matrix-style survey questions.
body: Spider Widget Overview and usage guide for the Spider Widget feature in the BI dashboard for matrix-style survey questions. Spider Widget Introduction Spider Widget is a data visualization feature in the BI dashboard designed for matrix-style survey questions, where multiple attributes or statements are measured using the same response scale (such as a Likert scale, awareness, or satisfaction level). It presents data in a radial chart—also known as a radar or spider chart—allowing users to easily compare multiple attributes on a unified scale. This feature is particularly effective for showcasing perception, performance, or sentiment across various categories, enabling quick visual assessment of strengths, weaknesses, and overall balance among attributes. Core Functionality Key Capabilities of the Spider Feature: Matrix Question Compatibility: Each axis on the chart represents one attribute, and the plotted values show the distribution or average score across those attributes. Radial Comparison Visualization: Data is displayed as a closed polygon across the axes, making it easier to spot patterns, imbalances, or outliers in responses. Multiple Series Support: Users can overlay multiple series on the same chart—ideal for comparing data across time periods, respondent groups, or survey waves. Customizable Scales and Labels: Adjust response ranges and axis labels to fit different use cases, such as satisfaction, agreement, or importance. Common Tasks: Compare brand perceptions across different traits (e.g., trust, value, innovation). Track employee satisfaction scores by department or location. Analyze change in customer sentiment across survey waves or after product updates. Step By Step Guide BI > Create Dashboard BI > Create Dashboard > Blank Dashboard BI > Create Dashboard > Blank Dashboard > Add Widget BI > Create Dashboard > Blank Dashboard > Add Widget > Question Based BI > Create Dashboard > Blank Dashboard > Add Widget > Question Based > Select Survey BI > Create Dashboard > Blank Dashboard > Add Widget > Question Based > Select Survey > Select Survey Question (Type - Matrix) BI > Create Dashboard > Blank Dashboard > Add Widget > Question Based > Select Survey > Select Survey Question (Type - Matrix) > Spider Widget BI > Spider Widget Glossary Matrix Question: A survey format where multiple attributes are rated on the same response scale. Spider Chart / Radar Chart: A graphical method of displaying multivariate data using axes starting from the same point. Attribute: A specific trait, statement, or item being evaluated in a matrix question. Likert Scale: A type of rating scale used to represent levels of agreement, frequency, importance, etc. Series: A group of related data points plotted on the same chart for comparison (e.g., Wave 1 vs. Wave 2). summary: Graphical representation of matrix data.
body: Matrix bar Graphical representation of matrix data. Matrix bar The matrix Bar is a data visualization widget in the BI dashboard designed for matrix-style questions or any question containing multiple rows with common categorical columns. It enables users to visualize data using averages, counts, or average percentages across different rows, providing clear insights into comparative patterns. How do I add a Matrix Bar? To add a Matrix Bar, follow these steps: Click on Add Widget. Select the desired data source. Choose a matrix-style question. Select the Matrix Bar widget and confirm by clicking Add Widget. Different chart metrics A chart metric refers to the data used to generate a chart. The following chart metrics are available for use: Count: Based on the number of respondents for that row. Percentage: Based on the mean percentage for that row. Mean: Based on the mean for that row. License This feature is available with the following licenses : Team Edition Research Edition Communities Customer Experience Employee Experience University - Research Edition Was the article helpful? Stat Highlight Introduction The Stat Highlight widget allows you to create a single-value statistics widget to display key survey metrics such as Net Promoter Score (NPS), Customer Effort Score (CES), Customer Satisfaction Score (CSAT), Mean, and Mean Percentage. This widget is designed to surface important performance indicators at a glance, making it ideal for dashboards focused on high-level insights and quick interpretation. To use the Stat Highlight widget, add it as a standard question-based widget to a BI dashboard, select a data source, and configure the desired scoring model and display settings. Stat Highlight widget displays key survey metrics such as NPS, CES, CSAT, and averages as a single highlighted value.

Stat Highlight Introduction The Stat Highlight widget allows you to create a single-value statistics widget to display key survey metrics such as Net Promoter Score (NPS), Customer Effort Score (CES), Customer Satisfaction Score (CSAT), Mean, and Mean Percentage. This widget is designed to surface important performance indicators at a glance, making it ideal for dashboards focused on high-level insights and quick interpretation. To use the Stat Highlight widget, add it as a standard question-based widget to a BI dashboard, select a data source, and configure the desired scoring model and display settings. Watch this video for a quick overview of the Stat Highlight widget in BI: Your browser does not support the video tag. Click to download the video Core Functionality Single-Value Metric Display Displays a single calculated statistic derived from survey responses to highlight key performance indicators. Support for Standard Scoring Models Supports commonly used customer experience metrics such as NPS, CES, CSAT, Mean, and Mean Percentage. Custom Scoring Configuration Allows mapping of answer options to scoring categories based on the selected scoring model. Flexible Number Formatting Provides options to control decimal precision, number formats, prefixes, suffixes, and value labels. Filter-Aware Calculations Dynamically updates the displayed value based on applied dashboard or widget-level filters. Common Tasks: Highlight key customer experience metrics such as NPS, CES, or CSAT. Display average or percentage-based scores for survey questions. Apply custom scoring logic to categorical response options. Add contextual labels, prefixes, or suffixes to improve clarity. Control how filters impact the displayed statistic. Step By Step Guide to creating a Stat Highlight widget: Step 1: Select the dashboard where the widget will be added. Navigate to BI and select the dashboard from the dashboard list where you want to add the Stat Highlight widget. Step 2: Start adding a new widget to the dashboard. On the selected dashboard, click the Add widget button located in the top-right area of the screen. Step 3: Select question based widget type. Stat Highlight is part of the standard question-based widgets available in BI. Step 4: Select the data source. Choose the data source for which you want to create the Stat Highlight widget. Step 5: Select Stat Highlight and provide a widget name. From the widget list, select Stat Highlight and enter a name for the widget in the provided name field. Stat Highlight General settings: How to add a highlighted insight to the Stat Highlight widget? Enable the highlighted insight option in the General tab and enter your message in the provided input field to display a short explanatory text above the value. How to control how filters apply to the Stat Highlight widget? Use the filter options in the General tab to decide whether dashboard filters, widget filters, or no filters should apply. How to apply a value label to the Stat Highlight widget? Use the value label field in the General tab to define a label displayed below the metric value. How to add a prefix or suffix? Use the Prefix and Suffix fields in the General tab to add contextual symbols or text to the displayed value. How to change the number format? Select the desired number format from the Number format dropdown, such as International, European, Indian, Compact, or No formatting. Stat Highlight Analytics settings: How to enable widget stats? From the Analytics tab, enable the Widget stats toggle to display additional information such as response count. How to change the customer scoring model? Use the Customer scoring model dropdown in the Analytics tab and select the desired scoring model for the widget. Glossary Stat Highlight Widget: A BI dashboard widget that displays a single calculated statistic derived from survey responses. Customer Scoring Model: A predefined calculation logic used to derive metrics such as NPS, CES, or CSAT by mapping responses to scoring categories. Widget Stats: Additional contextual information, such as response count, displayed alongside the primary metric. Number Format: A display option that controls how numeric values are formatted and presented in the widget. Highlighted Insight: A short explanatory message shown above the widget value to provide context or interpretation. License This feature is available with the following licenses : Team Edition Research Edition Communities Customer Experience Employee Experience

title: Map
kw: map dashboard widget bi region count create geographic survey responses percentage blank continue feature specific add collected surveys regions visual each total advanced dots settings response visualization shows participation displaying across locations provides focus areas helpful particular countries states many
summary: Map is a geographic visualization feature in the BI dashboard that shows survey participation by region.
body: Map Map is a geographic visualization feature in the BI dashboard that shows survey participation by region. Map Introduction Map is a geographic visualization feature available on the BI dashboard. It enables users to see where survey responses were collected by displaying respondent count on a map by count and percentage. This feature is especially useful when surveys are conducted across multiple locations or regions, as it provides a clear visual representation of participation density. You can focus on specific areas rather than viewing the entire world map—helpful when surveys are limited to particular countries, states, or regions. By using the Map feature, you gain quick insights into where your respondents are located, how many participated in each area, and the relative contribution of each region to the overall sample. Core Functionality Geographic Breakdown by Count and Percentage The map displays how many responses were collected from each region and what percentage they represent of the total sample. This is shown directly on the map through color intensity or tooltips. Region Selection You can focus the map on specific regions instead of displaying a global view. This makes it easier to analyze results from localized surveys or remove noise from irrelevant geographies. Visual Insight The map provides an immediate and intuitive understanding of where your survey had the most or least traction, making it ideal for stakeholder presentations or internal tracking. title: Response Timeline

summary: Response Timeline is a BI dashboard feature that visualizes how survey responses were collected over time.
body: Response Timeline Response Timeline is a BI dashboard feature that visualizes how survey responses were collected over time. Response Timeline Introduction Response Timeline is a time-series visualization feature in the BI dashboard that displays how survey responses were collected over a defined period. It provides a clear view of response trends by date, helping users understand data collection progress, identify response peaks and dips, and assess campaign effectiveness. The feature is particularly valuable for monitoring the pace of data collection in real time and making timely adjustments to fieldwork or outreach if necessary. Core Functionality Time-Based Response Tracking Visualizes how responses accumulate over time—daily, weekly, or monthly—allowing users to monitor the progress of data collection. Date Range Selection Customize the displayed date range to focus on specific periods during the survey fielding window. Volume Indicators Bars or line points represent the number of responses collected on each day, offering a quick way to spot surges or drop-offs. Real-Time Monitoring For live surveys, the timeline updates with the latest data, enabling teams to take immediate action if targets are not being met. Common Tasks: Monitor daily response volume. Identify days with peak survey participation. Filter to display a specific fielding period. Compare response trends across different time periods or waves. Real-Time Monitoring For live surveys, the timeline updates with the latest data, enabling teams to take immediate action if targets are not being met. Common Tasks: Monitor daily response volume. Identify days with peak survey participation. Filter to display a specific fielding period. Compare response trends across different time periods or waves. body: Response Info Response Info provides a quick summary of key survey response metrics such as total responses, completions, response time, and survey duration. Response Info Introduction Response Info is a metrics summary feature in the BI dashboard that provides key statistics about your survey’s data collection. It offers a snapshot of survey progress and quality by displaying the total number of responses, number of completed responses, average time taken, and more. This feature helps users quickly assess fieldwork status, identify outliers in response behavior, and validate data quality before further analysis. It is especially useful in tracking survey health and operational efficiency during live data collection. Core Functionality Total Responses Displays the total number of survey responses collected, including both completed and partials (if applicable). Completed Responses Shows how many respondents finished the entire survey, based on your survey completion logic. Average Response Time Calculates the mean time respondents took to complete the survey, helping evaluate survey length or complexity. Number of Questions Displays the total number of questions that respondents were exposed to during the survey. First and Last Response Date Indicates the date when the first and last responses were collected, offering a fieldwork duration view. Minimum and Maximum Response Time Shows the shortest and longest time taken by any respondent to complete the survey. Useful for spotting speeders or slow takers. Completed Responses: The number of respondents who reached the end of the survey as defined by the completion logic. Average Response Time: The average duration (in minutes or seconds) taken by all respondents to complete the survey. Number of Questions: The total count of questions shown to respondents based on the survey logic. First Response Date: The date on which the first response was submitted. Last Response Date: The date on which the most recent response was submitted. Min

summary: Comparative Bar help file for BI dashboard
body: Comparative Bar Comparative Bar help file for BI dashboard Comparative Bar Introduction Comparative Bar is a visualization feature in the BI dashboard that enables users to compare responses across multiple questions—either from the same survey or across different surveys. It uses side-by-side bar charts to visually present differences in data, making comparisons intuitive and easy to interpret. This feature is particularly useful for tracking changes over time, analyzing performance across regions or demographics, or comparing benchmarks between surveys. Whether you're looking to monitor trend shifts or validate consistency, the Comparative Bar offers a straightforward way to analyze multiple datasets in one view. Core Functionality Multi-Survey Comparison: Easily compare the same question across different surveys. This is ideal for longitudinal studies or benchmarking exercises. Same-Survey Multi-Question Comparison: Use Comparative Bar to view and compare different but related questions from a single survey side by side. Visual Simplicity: Bars are aligned and color-coded for clear visual distinction, making insights accessible at a glance. Common Tasks: Compare Net Promoter Scores (NPS) across two waves of research. Contrast satisfaction scores across different regions or demographics. Track changes in key metrics between two time points. title: Response Viewer

summary: Help documentation for the BI-Response-Viewer widget
body: Response Viewer Help documentation for the BI-Response-Viewer widget BI-Response-Viewer 1. Introduction The BI-Response-Viewer widget allows users to view individual-level survey responses directly within a dashboard environment. It provides a clean, scrollable, and paginated interface to inspect detailed response data without having to export or switch contexts. Purpose and Benefits: Enables analysts and stakeholders to view raw survey data inline with visual analytics. Supports quick diagnostics, audit trails, and verbatim review for text responses. Ideal for QA, storytelling, and deep dives into outlier or thematic segments. 2. Core Functionality Dashboard Integration: Add the widget as a visual block to any BI dashboard layout. Response Pagination: Navigate through all responses page by page. Horizontal Scroll: Easily scroll across all survey questions from left to right. Timestamp Display: View response time and date per entry, supporting audit needs. Response Filtering: Filter the Response Viewer data by applying column-level filters on available Questions and BI variables. 3. Step By Step Guide Step 1: Create dashboard – To create response view widget create a new dashboard or in an existing one, go to Step 6. Step 2: Select blank dashboard – Choose a blank dashboard layout as the starting point for adding your response view widget. Step 3: Add widget – Click on “Add Widget” to begin inserting response view into your dashboard. Step 4: Select advanced widget – User need to select advanced widget to add response view. Step 5: Select response view widget – User can select response view (table) and add to dashboard. Step 6: Select survey – User need to indicate which survey dataset needs to be added as a widget to the dashboard. Step 7a: Right arrow (Page next) – User choose to review the response by moving to the next page in the widget. Step 7b: Horizontal scroll – User choose to review all responses and questions in the survey. Step 8: Settings > Columns – User can choose columns they would like to add in the response view widget. Metadata: Choose to display survey metadata in the widget. Questions: Choose to display survey questions in the widget. System variables: Choose to display system variables in the widget. BI variables: Display BI variables mapped to the survey.Coming soon Step 9: Response filtering – User can filter the Response Viewer data by applying column-level filters on available Questions and BI variables. Click the filter icon next to a Question or BI variable column header. Select one or more response values to refine the data shown in the widget. These filters are temporary and apply only to the current session. Filters do not persist across page refreshes or shared dashboards. Glossary Response Viewer: A dashboard component that shows row-level survey response data. Pagination: Breaking the data into multiple pages for easy navigation. Scroll: Moving horizontally to see all available columns/questions. BI Dashboard: A

summary: Funnel Chart is an advanced BI widget that visualizes segmented survey responses using counts or percentages.
body: Funnel Chart Funnel Chart is an advanced BI widget that visualizes segmented survey responses using counts or percentages. Funnel Chart Introduction Funnel Chart is an advanced visualization widget in the BI dashboard that displays how survey responses are distributed across multiple segments. It allows users to compare response counts or percentages in a funnel-style layout. This widget is especially useful for analyzing relative proportions, identifying drop-offs between segments, and comparing segmented response behavior using flexible calculation and visualization options. To use the Funnel Chart, add it as an Advanced Widget to a BI dashboard, select a survey as the data source, and define one or more segments. Additional analytics and segment settings can be configured after the widget is created. Watch this video for a quick overview of funnel charts in BI: Your browser does not support the video tag. Click to download the video Core Functionality Segment-Based Visualization Displays survey responses grouped by user-defined segments in a funnel layout. Flexible Metric Selection View data as absolute counts or percentages based on configurable calculation logic. Custom Percentage Calculation Define global or per-segment denominator logic for percentage calculations. Advanced Display Controls Customize chart orientation, tooltip behavior, and weighting schemes. Common Tasks: Compare response distribution across multiple segments. Analyze proportional differences using percentage-based funnels. Apply custom denominator logic for accurate comparisons. Adjust chart layout and interaction behavior. Step By Step Guide to creating a Funnel Chart widget: Step 1: Select the dashboard where the widget will be added. Navigate to BI and select the dashboard from the dashboard list where you want to add the Funnel Chart. Step 2: Start adding a new widget to the dashboard. On the selected dashboard, click the Add widget button located in the top-right area of the screen. Step 3: Open the list of advanced widgets. In the Add Widget dialog, select Advanced Widgets to view advanced visualization options. Step 4: Select the Funnel Chart and provide a widget name. From the Advanced Widgets list, select Funnel Chart and enter a name for the widget in the name field provided. Step 5: Choose the survey data source. Select the survey that will be used as the data source for the Funnel Chart. Step 6: Add an initial segment and create the widget. Define a segment using the available conditions, then click Save widget to create the Funnel Chart. Funnel chart General settings: How to add a highlighted insight to the Funnel Chart? Enable the highlighted insight option in the General tab and add your message in the provided input field to display a short explanatory text above the chart. How to control how filters apply to the Funnel Chart? Use the filter options in the General tab to decide whether dashboard filters, widget filters, or no filters should apply. How to
content:
Funnel Chart

Funnel Chart is an advanced BI widget that visualizes segmented survey responses using counts or percentages.

Funnel Chart Introduction Funnel Chart is an advanced visualization widget in the BI dashboard that displays how survey responses are distributed across multiple segments. It allows users to compare response counts or percentages in a funnel-style layout. This widget is especially useful for analyzing relative proportions, identifying drop-offs between segments, and comparing segmented response behavior using flexible calculation and visualization options. To use the Funnel Chart, add it as an Advanced Widget to a BI dashboard, select a survey as the data source, and define one or more segments. Additional analytics and segment settings can be configured after the widget is created. Watch this video for a quick overview of funnel charts in BI: Your browser does not support the video tag. Click to download the video Core Functionality Segment-Based Visualization Displays survey responses grouped by user-defined segments in a funnel layout. Flexible Metric Selection View data as absolute counts or percentages based on configurable calculation logic. Custom Percentage Calculation Define global or per-segment denominator logic for percentage calculations. Advanced Display Controls Customize chart orientation, tooltip behavior, and weighting schemes. Common Tasks: Compare response distribution across multiple segments. Analyze proportional differences using percentage-based funnels. Apply custom denominator logic for accurate comparisons. Adjust chart layout and interaction behavior. Step By Step Guide to creating a Funnel Chart widget: Step 1: Select the dashboard where the widget will be added. Navigate to BI and select the dashboard from the dashboard list where you want to add the Funnel Chart. Step 2: Start adding a new widget to the dashboard. On the selected dashboard, click the Add widget button located in the top-right area of the screen. Step 3: Open the list of advanced widgets. In the Add Widget dialog, select Advanced Widgets to view advanced visualization options. Step 4: Select the Funnel Chart and provide a widget name. From the Advanced Widgets list, select Funnel Chart and enter a name for the widget in the name field provided. Step 5: Choose the survey data source. Select the survey that will be used as the data source for the Funnel Chart. Step 6: Add an initial segment and create the widget. Define a segment using the available conditions, then click Save widget to create the Funnel Chart. Funnel chart General settings: How to add a highlighted insight to the Funnel Chart? Enable the highlighted insight option in the General tab and add your message in the provided input field to display a short explanatory text above the chart. How to control how filters apply to the Funnel Chart? Use the filter options in the General tab to decide whether dashboard filters, widget filters, or no filters should apply. How to show or hide chart legends? Enable legends in the General tab to help users identify each segment in the funnel. How to configure data labels in the Funnel Chart? Choose whether data labels appear inside the chart or are hidden using the data label options. Funnel chart Analytics settings: How to switch between count and percentage metrics? Open the Analytics tab and use the Chart Metric option to switch between count and percentage. How to change the orientation of the Funnel Chart? In the Analytics tab, select Horizontal or Vertical under Chart Orientation. How to customize tooltip information? Use the Tooltip Style option in the Analytics tab to customize the information shown on hover. How to disable tooltips? Select None under Tooltip Style to hide tooltips. How to apply weighting at the widget level? In the Analytics tab, change the Weighting Scheme to Widget level. How to disable weighting? Select None under Weighting Scheme to display unweighted data. Funnel chart Segment settings: How to open segment settings for the Funnel Chart? Click the three-dot menu on the Funnel Chart widget and select Settings, then open the Segment tab. How to add more segments in a Funnel Chart? In the Segment tab, click the Create segment button to define additional segments. How to ensure responses belong to only one segment? Enable the Funnel segments mutual exclusive toggle to prevent overlap between segments. How to control percentage calculation logic? Turn on Custom segment percentage calculation mode to define how percentages are calculated. How to define a denominator segment? Use the Add option under Denominator Segment to select a reference segment for percentage calculations. How to apply different denominator logic for each segment? Enable Custom for each segment to assign different denominator segments per segment. Funnel chart Design settings: How to switch between dashboard and widget design types? Open the Design tab and choose whether the Funnel Chart should inherit dashboard styling or use widget-level styling. How to apply a different theme or color palette? After selecting widget-level design, choose a theme and color palette to customize the Funnel Chart’s appearance. Glossary Funnel Chart: An advanced BI visualization that displays how survey responses are distributed across multiple segments, helping users compare proportions and identify drop-offs between groups. General Settings: Controls that manage the overall behavior and readability of the Funnel Chart, such as highlighted insights, filter application, legends, and data labels. Analytics Settings: Options that define how data is calculated and displayed in the Funnel Chart, including metric type, chart orientation, tooltip behavior, and weighting. Segment Settings: Tools used to create, manage, and structure response segments, including rules for exclusivity and percentage calculation logic. Design Settings: Customization options that control the visual appearance of the Funnel C

title: Word Cloud

summary: Help documentation for the Word Cloud feature, used for visualizing frequently mentioned words in open-ended text data.
body: Word Cloud Help documentation for the Word Cloud feature, used for visualizing frequently mentioned words in open-ended text data. Word Cloud How does this work? Word Cloud GIF 1. Introduction The Word Cloud is a visual tool that displays the most frequently used words or phrases in open-ended text responses. Larger words represent higher frequency, offering users an immediate sense of prominent themes or topics in qualitative feedback. This feature helps users quickly identify dominant terms, support storytelling with visuals, and dig deeper into sentiment and keyword trends. 2. Core Functionality Auto-generated Frequency-Based Clouds – Automatically identifies and visualizes frequently used terms across all or filtered responses. Stop Word Filtering – Removes common, non-informative words to highlight meaningful content. Users can also add custom stop words. Frequency Threshold Control – Lets users set a minimum frequency for words to be displayed in the cloud. 3. Step-by-Step Guide Step 1: Create dashboard – To create word cloud widget create a new dashboard or in an existing go to Step 6. Step 2: Select blank dashboard – Choose a blank dashboard layout as the starting point for adding your word cloud widget or in an existing go to Step 6. Step 3: Add widget – Click on “Add Widget” to begin inserting visual elements, such as the word cloud, into your dashboard Step 4: Select your widget – Choose the Word Cloud widget and link it to a specific open-ended survey question to visualize key terms from respondent feedback. Step 5: Select survey and survey question – Select the relevant survey and then choose a text-based (open-ended) question. The Word Cloud widget supports only text question types to generate meaningful visualizations from respondent verbatim. Step 6: Select word cloud widget – From the list of available widgets, select Word Cloud to create a visual representation of the most frequently used words in responses to the selected text question. Step 7: Change setting for word cloud widget – Click on the three-dot menu (⋮) on the Word Cloud widget to access and customize settings such as minimum word count threshold, excluded words (stop words), and other visualization preferences. Step 7a: Change setting for minimum no of words – Users can define a minimum word count threshold to ensure that only verbatim responses containing a sufficient number of words are included in the word cloud visualization. This serves as a quality control measure to maintain the relevance and reliability of the word cloud output. Step 7b: Change setting for stop words – Users can specify words they wish to exclude from appearing in the word cloud. These are commonly referred to as 'stop words'—terms that are either irrelevant, overly frequent, or non-informative (e.g., 'the', 'and', 'it'), and are typically removed to enhance the clarity and significance of the visualization. Step 8: Final word cloud widget – The final word cloud widget displays the most freq

Step 8: Final word cloud widget – The final word cloud widget displays the most frequently occurring and relevant terms from the selected verbatim responses, based on applied filters and settings. It offers a visual summary of key themes, enabling quick insights into common topics and sentiments expressed by respondents. 5. Glossary Word Cloud – A visual representation of word frequency in open-ended text responses. Stop Words – Common, low-information words (e.g., “and,” “the”) that are removed from the word cloud. summary: Learn how to use the Text Response Widget to read, search, and analyze open-ended survey responses with sentiment tagging.
body: Text Response Learn how to use the Text Response Widget to read, search, and analyze open-ended survey responses with sentiment tagging. Text Response 1. Introduction The Text Response Widget allows users to view and analyze verbatim responses to open-ended questions in a survey. It enables deeper insights by offering keyword-based filtering and sentiment tagging, making it easier to detect customer intent and tone. 2. Core Functionality View all verbatim responses for a selected open-ended question. Search for specific keywords to filter relevant responses. Enable automated sentiment detection to classify each response as Positive, Neutral, or Negative. Video to be added soon.... 3. Step By Step Guide Step 1: Create dashboard – To create text response widget create a new dashboard or in an exsisting go to Step 6. Step 2: Select blank dashboard – Choose a blank dashboard layout as the starting point for adding your text response widget or in an exsisting go to Step 6. Step 3: Add widget – Click on “Add Widget” to begin inserting visual elements, into your dashboard Step 4: Select your widget – Choose the text response widget and link it to a specific open-ended survey question to visualize verbatim from each respondent in the survey. Step 5: Select survey and survey question – Select the relevant survey and then choose a text-based (open-ended) question. The text response widget supports only text question types to display respondent verbatim. Step 6: Select text response widget – This widget is designed to display all verbatim responses associated with a selected open-ended survey question. It provides options to search specific keywords and optionally enable sentiment tagging for each response. Step 7: Text response widget added to dashboard – Once selected, the text response widget will be added to your dashboard, displaying all text responses linked to the chosen open-ended question Step 8: Search keywords in text response – Use the search bar to filter and display only those text responses that contain specific keywords of interest. Step 9: Highlight Sentiment – Click the three-dot menu (⋮) on the widget, go to Settings > Analytics, and enable "Highlight Sentiment" to display detected sentiment for each response. Step 10: Final text response widget – The widget is now fully configured and displays all open-ended responses, along with keyword filters and optional sentiment highlights for easier analysis. Glossary un ordered list Verbatim Response: A respondent’s exact written answer to an open-ended survey question. Keyword Search: A search tool to filter and display responses that contain specific keywords or phrases. Sentiment Detection: An automated system that classifies each response as Positive, Neutral, or Negative based on tone and language. Open-Ended Question: A type of survey question that allows respondents to write their own answers freely. Was the article helpful? Yes No How can we improve it? Submit Thank you for submitting fee

title: Sentiment Stackbar

summary: Visualize proportion of sentiments in open-ended question responses using a stacked bar format.
body: Sentiment Stackbar Visualize proportion of sentiments in open-ended question responses using a stacked bar format. Sentiment Stackbar Help File 1.Introduction The Sentiment Stackbar visualizes the sentiment distribution across responses to an open-ended survey question. It helps users understand the overall emotional tone expressed in text comments by showing the proportion of each sentiment—ranging from very negative to very positive—in a single stacked bar. 2.Core Functionality The Sentiment Stackbar provides a proportional visual breakdown of sentiments detected in text responses for a selected question. Very Negative Negative Neutral Positive Very Positive No Sentiment Each sentiment is represented as a color-coded segment of a single horizontal bar, where the total length equals 100% of responses. It provides quick insight into emotional trends in your data. This visualization is ideal for comparing sentiment across time periods, demographics, or filtered data sets. 3.Step By Step Guide Step 1: Create dashboard – To create sentiment stackbar response widget create a new dashboard or in an existing go to Step 6. Step 2: Select blank dashboard – Choose a blank dashboard layout as the starting point for adding your sentiment stackbar widget or in an existing go to Step 6. Step 3: Add widget – Click on “Add Widget” to begin inserting visual elements, into your dashboard Step 4: Select your widget – Choose the sentiment stackbar widget and link it to a specific open-ended survey question to visualize sentiment proportion from each respondent in the survey. Step 5: Select survey and survey question – Select the relevant survey and then choose a text-based (open-ended) question. The sentiment stackbar widget supports only text question types to display proportion of sentiments for a specific survey question. Step 6: Select sentiment stackbar – Select the relevant survey and then choose a text-based (open-ended) question. The sentiment stackbar widget supports only text question types to display proportion of sentiment for a specific survey question. Step 7: Final sentiment stackbar widget – The sentiment stackbar widget is now added to the dashboard, displaying the proportion of sentiments—very negative, negative, neutral, positive, very positive, or no sentiment—for the selected question. This visual summary helps users quickly interpret the emotional tone of responses at a glance. Glossary Sentiment Analysis: An NLP technique used to identify the emotional tone in textual data. Stacked Bar Chart: A chart where different segments represent different parts of a whole. No Sentiment: Indicates that no clear sentiment could be detected in the response. Was the article helpful? Yes No How can we improve it? Submit Thank you for submitting feedback.

summary: Help file for the Sentiment Donut widget that displays sentiment proportions in a donut chart format.
body: Sentiment Donut Help file for the Sentiment Donut widget that displays sentiment proportions in a donut chart format. Sentiment Donut 1. Introduction The Sentiment Donut is a visual widget that shows the proportional distribution of sentiments derived from text responses to a specific survey question. This donut-style chart categorizes responses into six sentiment categories: Very Negative, Negative, Neutral, Positive, Very Positive, and No Sentiment. It helps users quickly assess the emotional tone of feedback and compare sentiment trends across questions, groups, or time periods. 2. Core Functionality Sentiment Categories: Each sentiment is color-coded and represented as a segment of the donut. Very Negative Negative Neutral Positive Very Positive No Sentiment Proportional View: The donut visually represents the percentage share of each sentiment based on the total number of valid text responses. Cross-Question Comparison: Multiple sentiment donuts can be used to compare sentiment across groups or questions (if available). Video coming soon... 3. Step By Step Guide Step 1: Create dashboard – To create sentiment donut widget create a new dashboard or in an existing go to Step 6. Step 2: Select blank dashboard – Choose a blank dashboard layout as the starting point for adding your sentiment donut widget or in an existing go to Step 6. Step 3: Add widget – Click on “Add Widget” to begin inserting visual elements, into your dashboard Step 4: Select your widget – Choose the sentiment donut widget and link it to a specific open-ended survey question to visualize sentiment proportion from each respondent in the survey. Step 5: Select survey and survey question – Select the relevant survey and then choose a text-based (open-ended) question. The sentiment donut widget supports only text question types to display proportion of sentiments for a specific survey question. Step 6: Select sentiment donut – Select the relevant survey and then choose a text-based (open-ended) question. The sentiment donut widget supports only text question types to display proportion of sentiment for a specific survey question. Step 7: Change setting for sentiment donut widget – Click on the three-dot menu (⋮) on the sentiment donut widget to access and customize settings for the widget Step 7a: Change setting > Analytics – Select to add or remove smiley form the widget, overall sentiment score or add response count Step 8: Final sentiment donut widget – This widget visually displays the proportion of sentiments—Very Negative, Negative, Neutral, Positive, Very Positive, or No Sentiment—derived from the analyzed text responses to a specific question, using a color-coded donut chart. It provides an at-a-glance summary of overall respondent sentiment. title: Topic Stacked Bar
kw: sentiment topic step bar stackbar widget very positive negative stacked users responses chart select click open ended dashboard each neutral survey question comments distribution topics segments drill score line overall view create choose visualization analysis across different format represents stack
summary: Help file for Topic Stackbar visualization in sentiment analysis.
body: Topic Stacked Bar Help file for Topic Stackbar visualization in sentiment analysis. Topic Stackbar 1. Introduction The Topic Stackbar is a visualization tool that displays the distribution of sentiment across different topics in a stacked bar format. Each bar represents a topic, and the stack segments within the bar indicate the proportion of sentiments — Very Positive, Positive, Neutral, Negative, and Very Negative. This feature helps users quickly compare sentiment distribution across topics and drill down into the actual responses behind each sentiment. Additionally, a sentiment score line chart overlays the bar chart as a secondary axis, helping users assess overall topic sentiment trends at a glance. 2. Core Functionality Stacked Sentiment Distribution: Each topic is represented as a bar, stacked with different sentiment layers (Very Positive, Positive, Neutral, Negative, Very Negative). Interactive Drill-down: Users can click on any sentiment layer within a bar to view the underlying open-ended responses tagged to that sentiment and topic. Tooltips and Highlights: Hovering over a stack or the sentiment line reveals the exact percentage and count of responses for that sentiment level. Filtering Options: Users can filter topics, time periods, or respondent segments using BI filters to tailor the analysis context. 3. Step By Step Guide Step 1: Create dashboard – To create Topic stackbar widget create a new dashboard or in an existing one, go to Step 6. Step 2: Select blank dashboard – Choose a blank dashboard layout as the starting point for adding your Topic stackbar widget. Step 3: Add widget – Click on “Add Widget” to begin inserting visual elements into your dashboard. Step 4: Select your widget – Choose the Topic stackbar widget and link it to a specific open-ended survey question. Step 5: Select survey and question – Select the relevant survey and choose a text-based (open-ended) question. Step 6: Select stackbar – Reconfirm your survey and open-ended question to configure the widget properly. Step 7: Topic stackbar widget – The widget visually breaks down sentiment by topic in a stacked bar format. Each bar represents a topic, with color-coded segments showing proportions of Very Positive, Positive, Neutral, Negative, and Very Negative responses. A line chart overlays the bars to display the overall sentiment score per topic. Step 8: Click stackbar for comment – Users can click on any sentiment segment to view underlying comments. Step 9: List of comments – Browse through comments and their assigned sentiments. Step 10: Select page to view next set of comments – Use pagination to navigate through more responses. 4. Glossary Sentiment: A classification of respondent tone as Very Positive, Positive, Neutral, Negative, or Very Negative. Topic: A keyword or theme identified in open-ended responses through automated or manual tagging. Stacked Bar Chart: A chart type where individual categories (sentiment levels) are stacked on top of one ano

title: Create a data filter

summary: Creating filters for your dashboard
body: Create a data filter Creating filters for your dashboard Create filters for your data What is a filter? Data filter is a smaller part of your data which you can use as a subset for viewing, analysis or comparison. Filtering allows you to view / analyze data based on certain key parameters or criteria. How to create a data filter? Go to: Login » Select dashboard » Filter » Response status and date range » Add criteria » Save filter » Name » Save Click to download video Step 1 Once you are inside your dashboard, click on Filter » Chose response status and date range » Add criteria Step 2 Add the required rules » Save filter You can create a maximum of 10 rules in 1 criteria How can I apply a filter to the entire dashboard? When you create a new filter, it is automatically applied to the dashboard. To apply an older filter, follow the following steps: Filter » Open the create new filter dropdown » Select the filter How can I apply a filter to a specific tab in the dashboard? To apply a filter to a specific tab on the dashboard, follow the following steps - Click on the 3 dots next to the tab » Tab filter » Select the filter » Save What is a default filter? Default filter is automatically applied to the dashboard. License This feature is available with the following licenses : Team Edition Research Edition Communities Customer Experience Employee Experience Was the article helpful? Yes No How can we improve it? Submit Thank you for submitting feedback.

summary: Create data slicers for your dashboard
body: Data slicer Create data slicers for your dashboard Adding data slicers What is a data slicer? Data slicers are different slices or segments of your data, which you can use to visually compare the data against one or more slicers. How to add a data slicer? Go to: Dashboard settings » Data slicers » Create data slicer » Give it a name » Add the criteria » Save » Select the slicer you wish to use. Click to download video Step 1: Once you are inside your dashboard, click on Dashboard settings Step 2: Click on data slicers » Create data slicers Step 3: Give the slicer a name » add criteria » Save Step 4: Select the slicer you wish to apply to the dashboard How to apply or disable a slicer from a specific widget? Data slicers can be applied to the entire dashboard, disabled from certain widgets or only be applied to certain widgets To update data slicer settings on a particular widget, follow the following steps: Step 1: Click on more and then settings for the widget Step 2: Analytics » Data Slicer options License This feature is available with the following licenses : Team Edition Research Edition Communities Customer Experience Employee Experience Was the article helpful? title: Exports

summary: BI Helpfile - Exports
body: Exports BI Helpfile - Exports BI - Exports Introduction The Exports feature within the QuestionPro platform provides a comprehensive solution for users seeking to analyze their survey data. By offering compatibility with widely adopted formats like SPSS (.sav) and CSV (.csv), Exports ensures seamless integration with a broad array of statistical and analytical tools. This functionality empowers users to delve deeper into their survey results, extracting valuable insights and facilitating data-driven decision-making. Purpose and Benefits: Streamline Data Analysis:Effortlessly transfer your survey data into a format that's primed for analysis, eliminating manual formatting and reducing the risk of errors. Ensure Broad Compatibility:Export your data in widely accepted formats that seamlessly integrate with popular statistical software packages like SPSS, R, and SAS, as well as spreadsheet programs like Excel. Accelerate Insights:Save valuable time and effort by generating clean, well-structured data exports that are ready for immediate use in your analysis tools, allowing you to focus on extracting meaningful insights. Core Functionality Export Format Selection CSV: This widely compatible format can be opened and edited in various spreadsheet software, including Microsoft Excel, Google Sheets, Numbers, and OpenOffice Calc. It's ideal for data analysis, manipulation, and sharing. SPSS: Specifically designed for statistical analysis, this format is compatible with IBM SPSS Statistics software. It preserves valuable metadata such as variable labels and value codes, ensuring data integrity and facilitating in-depth analysis within SPSS. Getting Started Coming Soon Steps for Setup BI -> Exports BI -> Exports -> Select format (CSV or SPSS) BI -> Exports -> Select format (CSV or SPSS) -> Select Survey BI -> Exports -> Select format (CSV or SPSS) -> Select Survey -> Download. Steps for Setup (Combined Surveys) BI -> Exports -> Survey -> My Survey Stack BI -> Exports -> Survey -> My Survey Stack -> Select Survey BI -> Exports -> Survey -> My Survey Stack -> Select Survey -> Download. Glossary CSV:Comma Separated Values, used for spreadsheets. SPSS: A statistical software format used for complex data analysis. Was the article helpful? Yes No How can we improve it? Submit Thank you for submitting feedback.

summary: BI Helpfile - Segment Trend
body: Survey Stack BI Helpfile - Segment Trend Survey Stack Introduction Survey Stacks is a data management feature that empowers users to seamlessly integrate data from multiple surveys into a single, cohesive dataset. This centralized repository of information facilitates streamlined reporting, in-depth analysis, and the generation of actionable insights. By merging data from various surveys, Survey Stacks eliminates the need for manual data consolidation and enables a holistic view of respondent feedback and behavior. This feature is particularly valuable for clients that conduct multiple surveys over time, across different departments, or for varying target audiences. With Survey Stacks, users can identify trends, compare results, and track changes in respondent sentiment over time. This comprehensive understanding of data can inform strategic decision-making, drive process improvements, and enhance overall organizational performance. Purpose and Benefits: Data Consolidation: This involves merging responses from multiple surveys into a unified dataset, allowing for a comprehensive analysis of collected information. Efficiency and Automation: Streamlining the data cleaning and merging process saves valuable time and reduces the need for manual intervention, minimizing the risk of errors and improving overall efficiency. Standardization: Ensuring consistency across different reporting systems enables seamless integration and facilitates accurate and reliable comparisons of data, leading to more informed decision-making. Core Functionality Browse and Select: Allows you to navigate through and choose the specific survey(s) that you want to include in your data merging process. It may display survey titles, creation dates, or other relevant information to help you identify the correct survey. Auto-Match Identical Fields: The system can automatically recognize and match fields that have the same name in different surveys, streamlining the merging process. Manual Field Name Override: If the automatic matching is incorrect or if you need to combine fields with different names, you can manually adjust and specify how the fields should be mapped.
